stories.title: Big Jon and Sparkie
stories.author: Jon Arthur
stories.summary: Big Jon and his elf friend Sparkie share stories, songs, and adventures — a Saturday morning children's show from the golden age of radio.
stories.description: No School Today (also known as Big Jon and Sparkie) was an ABC children's program starring Jon Arthur and a child-voiced mechanical elf named Sparkie. The show featured stories, music, and the ongoing serial 'General Comet of the Universe Patrol.' Pure Saturday-morning joy, designed specifically for children ages 4–12.
stories.authorBio: Jon 'Big Jon' Arthur was an American radio personality who hosted No School Today on ABC from 1950 to 1958. The show was one of the last great children's radio programs before television took over.
stories.relevance: Warm, gentle, and made for children — the radio equivalent of a favorite uncle who always has a story.
stories.coverScene: A tall, gentle-looking man sitting on a wooden fence under a spreading oak tree, holding a tiny elf on his knee. The elf has pointed ears, a green cap, and a mischievous grin. Fireflies glow in the meadow behind them. Warm twilight sky fading from gold to violet.

stories.title: Big Jon and Sparkie: Episode 2
stories.author: ABC Radio
stories.summary: Another episode of Big Jon and Sparkie — the children's radio variety show featuring stories, songs, and the adventures of Big Jon Arthur and his magical friend Sparkie.
stories.description: Big Jon and Sparkie (1950–1958) was an ABC Radio children's program hosted by Jon Arthur ('Big Jon') and featuring his puppet companion Sparkie. The show mixed storytelling, music, and gentle humor in a format designed for young listeners. It was warm, wholesome, and enormously popular with children of the 1950s.
stories.authorBio: Big Jon and Sparkie was an ABC Radio children's variety show (1950–1958) hosted by Jon Arthur. The show's mix of stories, music, and the relationship between Big Jon and his magical elfin companion Sparkie made it a beloved part of many American childhoods.
stories.relevance: Gentle, warm children's radio with stories and music. Big Jon and Sparkie represents the best of 1950s children's programming — wholesome without being dull, entertaining without being loud.
stories.coverScene: A tall man in a plaid shirt leaning against a barn door, laughing, while a tiny elf in a green cap dances on the fence rail beside him, arms outstretched for balance. A tabby cat watches from a hay bale. Late-afternoon sun casting long golden shadows across the farmyard.

stories.title: Big Jon and Sparkie: Episode 3
stories.author: ABC Radio
stories.summary: Another episode of Big Jon and Sparkie — the children's radio variety show featuring stories, songs, and the adventures of Big Jon Arthur and his magical friend Sparkie.

stories.title: The Cinnamon Bear: Paddy O'Cinnamon
stories.author: Glan Heisch
stories.summary: Twins Judy and Jimmy discover a tiny bear named Paddy O'Cinnamon who takes them on a magical Christmas adventure.
stories.description: It's nearly Christmas, and twins Judy and Jimmy climb to the attic to find the silver star for the tree — but it's missing. A toy cinnamon bear named Paddy O'Cinnamon comes to life and tells them the Crazy Quilt Dragon has stolen it. Together they shrink to toy-size and set off on a magical journey to get it back. Originally broadcast in 1937, this 26-part radio serial was a holiday tradition for decades.
stories.authorBio: Glan Heisch wrote and directed The Cinnamon Bear in 1937 for radio broadcast. The serial ran daily from Thanksgiving to Christmas and became one of the most beloved holiday radio programs in American history.
stories.relevance: A perfect Advent listen — one short episode per night from Thanksgiving to Christmas. Gentle enough for young children, magical enough for older ones.

series.name: The Cinnamon Bear
series.nextTeaser: Next: Judy, Jimmy, and Paddy meet Weary Willie on their quest for the silver star.

stories.coverScene: Two children — a boy and a girl, both about eight, in 1930s clothes — kneeling in a dusty attic beside a small cinnamon-colored teddy bear who stands upright on a steamer trunk, one paw pointing toward a shimmering crack of light in the attic wall. Cobwebs, old hat boxes, and a missing silver star ornament's empty hook on the rafters.

stories.title: The Cinnamon Bear: Weary Willie
stories.author: Glan Heisch
stories.summary: The children and Paddy O'Cinnamon encounter Weary Willie on their journey through Maybeland.
stories.description: Continuing their quest to recover the silver star from the Crazy Quilt Dragon, Judy, Jimmy, and Paddy travel through Maybeland and meet Weary Willie. Each 15-minute episode introduces a new character and a new challenge, building toward Christmas Day.
stories.authorBio: Glan Heisch wrote and directed The Cinnamon Bear in 1937 for radio broadcast.
stories.relevance: Short, self-contained episodes that end on gentle cliffhangers — ideal for one-per-night bedtime listening.

series.name: The Cinnamon Bear
series.nextTeaser: Next: The Crazy Quilt Dragon makes his first appearance.

stories.coverScene: Two children in 1930s clothes and a small upright cinnamon-colored teddy bear walking along a candy-cane path through a pastel landscape of lollipop trees and gumdrop bushes. A sleepy-looking scarecrow made of patchwork quilts leans against a signpost ahead of them. Soft, dreamy light as if seen through frosted glass.

stories.title: The Greatest Story Ever Told: The Good Samaritan
stories.author: Fulton Oursler
stories.summary: The parable of the Good Samaritan — dramatized with reverence.
stories.description: The Greatest Story Ever Told was an ABC radio program (1947–1956) dramatizing the life of Jesus Christ, based on Fulton Oursler's bestselling book. These are reverent, beautifully produced dramatizations of the Gospels — the kind of thing that used to be on the radio on Sunday evenings.
stories.authorBio: Fulton Oursler (1893–1952) was an American journalist and author whose 1949 book The Greatest Story Ever Told became one of the best-selling religious books of the twentieth century.
stories.relevance: Gospel stories dramatized for the ear — a beautiful way to hear Scripture at bedtime.
stories.coverScene: A man in a simple brown robe kneeling on a dusty road beside a wounded traveler, gently lifting the traveler's head and offering a clay water jug. A donkey waits patiently nearby. Rocky Judean hillside in the background, warm low sun casting long shadows across the pale stone.

stories.title: The Greatest Story Ever Told: The Lost Sheep
stories.author: ABC Radio
stories.summary: Jesus tells the parable of the shepherd who leaves his ninety-nine sheep to search for the one that is lost — dramatized by the award-winning ABC Radio program The Greatest Story Ever Told.
stories.description: The Greatest Story Ever Told was an ABC Radio program (1947–1957) that dramatized episodes from the life of Christ and the parables. The productions are reverent, well-acted, and bring Scripture to life as living drama. The Lost Sheep is one of the most beloved parables — about a God who pursues the one who is lost.
stories.authorBio: The Greatest Story Ever Told (1947–1957) was an ABC Radio program that dramatized Biblical stories with professional actors, original music, and careful attention to Scripture. It was sponsored by the Goodyear Tire and Rubber Company and produced by Henry Denker.
stories.relevance: Scripture brought to life as audio drama. The parable of the Lost Sheep teaches that every single person matters to God — a truth that children need to hear and that this production communicates beautifully.
stories.coverScene: A shepherd in a rough wool cloak carrying a small lamb over his shoulders, picking his way down a rocky hillside at dusk. Behind him, a flock of white sheep waits on the ridge. One oil lamp glows far below in a stone village. Deep blue twilight sky with a single bright star.

stories.title: The Greatest Story Ever Told: The Second Mile
stories.author: Fulton Oursler
stories.summary: Jesus teaches about going beyond what is required — dramatized from the Gospels.

stories.coverScene: A young man in a plain tunic walking a dusty road beside a Roman soldier in a red cloak and bronze helmet, carrying the soldier's heavy pack on his own shoulders. The soldier looks surprised. Low hills dotted with olive trees stretch behind them in warm afternoon light.

stories.title: Let's Pretend: Beauty and the Beast
stories.author: CBS Radio
stories.summary: Beauty agrees to live with the Beast to save her father, and discovers that true love sees past appearances — a fairy tale dramatized by the CBS Radio Workshop's beloved children's program.
stories.description: Let's Pretend was a CBS Radio program that aired from 1934 to 1954, dramatizing fairy tales and children's stories with a full cast, original music, and lavish sound effects. It was one of the most beloved children's programs of the Golden Age of Radio, and its productions remain charming, well-acted, and genuinely entertaining.
stories.authorBio: Let's Pretend (1934–1954) was a CBS Radio program created by Nila Mack that dramatized fairy tales and children's stories. It won a Peabody Award in 1943 and was consistently rated among the best children's programs on radio. The show featured child actors, many of whom went on to significant careers.
stories.relevance: Professional radio dramatizations of classic fairy tales — complete with music, sound effects, and a full cast. These are fairy tales experienced as theater, and they bring the stories to life in a way that readings alone cannot.
stories.coverScene: A young woman in a blue gown standing in a candlelit great hall, one hand extended toward a large, shaggy beast who rises from a velvet chair. A single red rose in a glass dome glows on the table between them. Tall gothic windows behind them show a snow-covered garden at twilight.

stories.title: Let's Pretend: Bluebeard
stories.author: CBS Radio
stories.summary: A fairy tale about a mysterious husband and a forbidden room — dramatized with music and young actors.
stories.description: Let's Pretend was CBS's beloved Saturday morning children's program, running from the 1930s through 1954. Young professional actors dramatized classic fairy tales with full orchestral music. These are the original radio performances — warm, vivid, and made with genuine care for young listeners.
stories.authorBio: Let's Pretend (originally The Adventures of Helen and Mary) was created by Nila Mack for CBS Radio. It won the Peabody Award and was considered the gold standard for children's radio drama.
stories.relevance: Radio drama for bedtime — like a movie for the ears. Children who listen to these build their imagination muscles.
stories.coverScene: A young woman in a white dress holding a small golden key, standing before a heavy iron door at the end of a torchlit stone corridor. Her shadow stretches long behind her. The key glows faintly. Cobwebs cling to the door's hinges.

stories.title: Let's Pretend: Brother and Sister
stories.author: CBS Radio
stories.summary: A brother and sister flee into the forest — a fairy tale of loyalty and enchantment.

stories.author: CBS Radio
stories.summary: The CBS radio dramatization of Cinderella — the classic fairy tale brought to life with a full cast, music, and sound effects by the beloved children's program Let's Pretend.

stories.title: Let's Pretend: Jack and the Beanstalk
stories.author: CBS Radio
stories.summary: Jack trades the cow for magic beans and climbs the beanstalk to a giant's castle in the clouds — dramatized with music and sound effects by the Let's Pretend radio company.

stories.title: Let's Pretend: The Snow Queen
stories.author: CBS Radio
stories.summary: Gerda sets out to rescue her friend Kai from the Snow Queen's frozen palace — Andersen's fairy tale brought to life by the CBS Radio children's program Let's Pretend.

Her breath hangs in the frozen air. A single reindeer stands behind her, waiting. Northern lights shimmer faintly overhead.

stories.title: Let's Pretend: The Enchanted Frog
stories.author: CBS Radio
stories.summary: A princess makes a promise to a frog — and finds out that promises must be kept.

stories.coverScene: A princess in a golden dress kneeling by a mossy well in a forest clearing, peering down at a small green frog sitting on the stone rim. The frog wears a tiny gold crown. Her golden ball rests at the bottom of the well, visible through clear water. Dappled afternoon sunlight.

stories.title: The Lone Ranger
stories.author: Fran Striker
stories.summary: A masked rider and his loyal companion Tonto bring justice to the Old West — with silver bullets and a moral code.
stories.description: The Lone Ranger was one of the most popular children's radio programs ever broadcast. The masked hero never shot to kill, always left a silver bullet as his calling card, and stood for fairness, justice, and the idea that one person could make a difference. Created at WXYZ Detroit in 1933, it taught virtue to a generation of American children.
stories.authorBio: Fran Striker (1903–1962) created The Lone Ranger for WXYZ radio in Detroit. He wrote over 150 stories for the show and established the Lone Ranger's strict moral code — including the rule that the hero never removes his mask.
stories.relevance: Adventure with a moral compass. The Lone Ranger taught children that strength and gentleness go together — that the strongest person in the room is the one who doesn't need to prove it.
stories.coverScene: A masked rider on a rearing white horse atop a rocky desert ridge at sunset. The rider wears a wide-brimmed hat and a black domino mask over his eyes, one hand raised. Beside him on a paint horse, a man with long dark hair watches the horizon. Orange and purple sky, silhouetted saguaro cactus.

A paint horse with a dark-haired rider races alongside. Red canyon walls tower on either side. Late-afternoon sun, dust cloud trailing behind.

stories.title: Challenge of the Yukon
stories.author: Fran Striker
stories.summary: Sergeant Preston of the Northwest Mounted Police and his lead sled dog Yukon King solve crimes in the frozen north.
stories.description: Challenge of the Yukon (later Sergeant Preston of the Yukon) was a radio adventure series set in the Canadian wilderness. Preston and his dog Yukon King patrol the Yukon during the Gold Rush, delivering justice in the last frontier. Created by the same team behind The Lone Ranger, it has the same strong moral backbone — with the added beauty of wilderness, sled dogs, and the northern lights.
stories.authorBio: Fran Striker and George Trendle created Challenge of the Yukon at WXYZ Detroit — the same station that gave the world The Lone Ranger and The Green Hornet.
stories.relevance: Adventure in the wild — dogs, snow, and justice. For children who love animals and the outdoors.
stories.coverScene: A Royal Canadian Mountie in a red coat and wide-brimmed hat standing on a snow-covered ridge, one hand shielding his eyes against the glare, a large Alaskan malamute sitting alert beside him. Behind them, a vast frozen wilderness of snow-covered pines and distant mountains under a pale winter sky.

The lead dog — a powerful malamute — pulls hard against the harness, ears flat. Pine trees along the trail are bent under heavy snow. White-out conditions, barely visible trail ahead.

A pair of snowshoes leans against a pine tree. Stars glitter in a deep indigo sky above the treeline. Northern wilderness silence.

stories.title: The Adventures of Superman
stories.author: Jerry Siegel & Joe Shuster
stories.summary: A baby from a dying planet is raised by kind farmers in Kansas — and grows up to fight for truth, justice, and the American way.
stories.description: The original Adventures of Superman radio serial, starring Bud Collyer. These are the stories that introduced Superman to millions of American children in the 1940s — heroic, earnest, and powered by the idea that strength should be used to help the weak. The radio Superman is simpler and more purely good than later versions.
stories.authorBio: Superman was created by Jerry Siegel and Joe Shuster, two Jewish-American teenagers from Cleveland, Ohio. He first appeared in Action Comics #1 in June 1938 and has been the template for the superhero ever since.
stories.relevance: The original superhero — a story about using power for good. The radio episodes are short, exciting, and end on cliffhangers that make children want to come back tomorrow night.
stories.coverScene: A broad-shouldered man in a blue suit and red cape standing on a rooftop ledge high above an Art Deco city at night, one fist on his hip, red cape billowing in the wind. The city below is a grid of glowing windows and tiny yellow taxicabs. A large full moon behind him.

stories.title: The Ant and the Grasshopper
stories.author: Aesop
stories.summary: The ant works all summer while the grasshopper sings — and winter comes for both.
stories.description: One of Aesop's most enduring fables, retold for children. These short stories have taught virtue to children for over two thousand years — not by lecturing, but by showing what happens when animals (who behave very much like people) make choices.
stories.authorBio: Aesop is the legendary author of the world's most famous collection of fables. Whether he was a real person — possibly a Greek storyteller living in the 6th century BCE — or a name attached to a tradition of folk wisdom, the fables themselves have shaped moral teaching for more than two thousand years.
stories.relevance: Aesop's fables are usually a child's first taste of stories that come with a quiet lesson tucked inside. They're short enough for a tired parent and a tireder child.
stories.coverScene: A small brown ant carrying a grain of wheat twice its size along a dirt path, passing a green grasshopper lounging on a blade of grass with a tiny fiddle tucked under one arm. Golden wheat field stretching behind them. Late summer light, warm and hazy.

stories.author: Aesop
stories.summary: A shepherd boy lies about wolves so often that no one believes him when a real wolf comes.

stories.title: The Fox and the Grapes
stories.author: Aesop
stories.summary: A short fable about a fox who decides the grapes he cannot reach must be sour anyway.
stories.description: One of Aesop's shortest and most enduring fables. A hungry fox spies a bunch of ripe grapes hanging from a vine, leaps for them again and again, and finally walks away muttering that they were probably sour to begin with. From this little story we get the everyday phrase 'sour grapes' — the way we dismiss the things we cannot have.
stories.authorBio: Aesop is the legendary author of the world's most famous collection of fables. Whether he was a real person — possibly a Greek storyteller and slave living in the 6th century BCE — or a name later attached to a tradition of folk wisdom, the fables themselves have shaped storytelling for more than two thousand years.
stories.relevance: Aesop's fables are usually a child's first taste of stories that come with a quiet lesson tucked inside. They're short enough for a tired parent and a tireder child, and they leave room to talk afterward.
stories.coverScene: A red fox standing on hind legs in a sun-dappled vineyard, one paw stretched toward a cluster of fat purple grapes hanging just out of reach from a wooden trellis. The fox's expression is determined, tail curled behind it. Warm golden afternoon light filtering through vine leaves.

stories.title: The North Wind and the Sun
stories.author: Aesop
stories.summary: The wind and the sun argue about who is stronger — and the sun wins by being gentle.
stories.description: One of Aesop's most enduring fables, retold for children. These short stories have taught virtue to children for over two thousand years — not by lecturing, but by showing what happens when animals (who behave very much like people) make choices.
stories.authorBio: Aesop is the legendary author of the world's most famous collection of fables. Whether he was a real person — possibly a Greek storyteller living in the 6th century BCE — or a name attached to a tradition of folk wisdom, the fables themselves have shaped moral teaching for more than two thousand years.
stories.relevance: Aesop's fables are usually a child's first taste of stories that come with a quiet lesson tucked inside. They're short enough for a tired parent and a tireder child.
stories.coverScene: A fierce blue wind with a scowling face blowing hard from the left, and a warm golden sun with a gentle smile beaming from the right, both directed at a traveler below who clutches his cloak tightly around himself on a country road. Trees bending in the gale on one side, wildflowers blooming in warmth on the other.

stories.title: The Tortoise and the Hare
stories.author: Aesop
stories.summary: Slow and steady wins the race — but only if you actually keep going.
stories.description: One of Aesop's most enduring fables, retold for children. These short stories have taught virtue to children for over two thousand years — not by lecturing, but by showing what happens when animals (who behave very much like people) make choices.

Whether he was a real person — possibly a Greek storyteller living in the 6th century BCE — or a name attached to a tradition of folk wisdom, the fables themselves have shaped moral teaching for more than two thousand years.
stories.relevance: Aesop's fables are usually a child's first taste of stories that come with a quiet lesson tucked inside. They're short enough for a tired parent and a tireder child.
stories.coverScene: A small brown tortoise plodding steadily along a country lane, head stretched forward with quiet determination. Far behind on the grassy verge, a brown hare sleeps curled under a tree, one long ear flopped over its eye. A distant finish-line ribbon hangs between two posts. Soft morning light.

stories.title: The Town Mouse and the Country Mouse
stories.author: Aesop
stories.summary: Two mice visit each other's homes and learn that safety and plenty don't live in the same house.
stories.description: One of Aesop's most enduring fables, retold for children. These short stories have taught virtue to children for over two thousand years — not by lecturing, but by showing what happens when animals (who behave very much like people) make choices.

Whether he was a real person — possibly a Greek storyteller living in the 6th century BCE — or a name attached to a tradition of folk wisdom, the fables themselves have shaped moral teaching for more than two thousand years.
stories.relevance: Aesop's fables are usually a child's first taste of stories that come with a quiet lesson tucked inside. They're short enough for a tired parent and a tireder child.
stories.coverScene: Two mice sitting on opposite sides of a checkered tablecloth spread on a tree stump — one sleek and well-groomed in a tiny waistcoat, the other plump and rustic with a straw hat. Between them, a feast of cheese, bread crusts, and a single acorn. A hedgerow cottage garden behind them, late afternoon.

stories.title: The Blue Fairy Book
stories.author: Andrew Lang
stories.summary: The first and most famous of Andrew Lang's twelve colour Fairy Books — fairy tales from around the world.
stories.description: Andrew Lang's Blue Fairy Book, published in 1889, was the first of twelve colour-coded collections that gathered fairy tales from every culture in the world. This first volume includes versions of Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Hansel and Gretel, Aladdin, and many others — drawn from French, German, Norse, and Arabian sources. It is the starting point for one of the great anthologies of children's literature.
stories.authorBio: Andrew Lang (1844–1912) was a Scottish poet, novelist, and literary critic who compiled the twelve Fairy Books between 1889 and 1910. They remain the most comprehensive collection of world fairy tales for young readers in English.
stories.relevance: The greatest fairy tale anthology ever compiled — and the first volume is the best place to start.
stories.coverScene: A princess with long golden hair asleep on a canopied bed draped in blue silk, a spindle lying on the floor beside her. Thorny rose vines have grown through the window and wrap around the bedposts. A prince in a short cloak stands in the doorway, one hand on the vine-covered frame. Dusty blue twilight filtering through the roses.

stories.title: The Story of Joan of Arc
stories.author: Andrew Lang
stories.summary: A peasant girl from Domrémy hears voices telling her to save France — and she does.
stories.description: Andrew Lang's retelling of the life of Joan of Arc for young readers. Lang was one of the great Victorian collectors of fairy tales, and he brings the same narrative skill to Joan's story — the voices, the Dauphin, the siege of Orléans, the betrayal, and the fire. It reads like a fairy tale because Joan's life was stranger than any fairy tale.
stories.authorBio: Andrew Lang (1844–1912) was a Scottish poet, novelist, and literary critic best known for his twelve coloured Fairy Books. He was also a serious historian who wrote biographies of Joan of Arc, Mary Queen of Scots, and John Knox.
stories.relevance: One of the great saint stories for children — a girl their own age who heard God and obeyed, and who changed the history of a nation.

series.name: The Story of Joan of Arc

stories.coverScene: A young peasant girl in a plain dress kneeling in a sunlit garden beside a stone cottage, hands clasped in prayer, face tilted upward with an expression of wonder. Behind her, a small French village with a church steeple. Soft golden light surrounds her like a halo. Sheep graze in a meadow beyond the garden wall.

stories.title: The Story of Joan of Arc
stories.author: Andrew Lang
stories.summary: Joan hears the voices of the saints and begins her mission — traveling to the Dauphin's court to tell him that God has sent her to save France.
stories.description: Andrew Lang's retelling of the life of St. Joan of Arc, written for young readers. Joan's story is one of the most extraordinary in all of history — a teenage girl who led armies, crowned a king, and was martyred for her faith. Lang tells it with clarity and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Andrew Lang (1844–1912) was a Scottish poet, novelist, and collector of fairy tales. His twelve 'Coloured' Fairy Books gathered stories from traditions around the world. He also wrote popular retellings of historical figures, including Joan of Arc.
stories.relevance: The story of a young woman whose courage, faith, and obedience to God changed the course of history. A saint's story told as the adventure it truly was.

series.name: The Story of Joan of Arc

stories.coverScene: A young woman in simple armor standing before a group of armored knights at a castle gate, holding a white banner with a fleur-de-lis. The knights look skeptical but the girl's expression is calm and certain. Stone fortress walls and a drawbridge behind them. Overcast sky, wind stirring the banner.

stories.title: Black Beauty
stories.author: Anna Sewell
stories.summary: A beautiful black horse with a white star on his forehead remembers his first home — a large pleasant meadow with a clear pond.
stories.description: The first chapter of Black Beauty — told in the horse's own voice. A gentle, virtuous animal remembers the kindness of his first master and his mother's advice to always do his best. Sewell wrote it to teach compassion for animals, and it has been teaching it ever since.
stories.authorBio: Anna Sewell (1820–1878) was an English novelist who wrote only one book — Black Beauty — in the last years of her life, while housebound by illness. It became one of the best-selling novels of all time and changed the way people treated horses.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.
stories.coverScene: A young black colt with a white star on his forehead standing in a green meadow beside his mother, a gentle brown mare. The colt's legs are slightly splayed, still new to standing. A stone farmhouse and wooden fence in the background. Soft English countryside light, white clouds, buttercups in the grass.

stories.title: Wee Ones' Bible Stories
stories.author: Anonymous
stories.summary: The great stories of the Bible told simply for the very youngest children.
stories.description: Bible stories retold in the simplest possible language for toddlers and very young children. The Creation, Noah's Ark, David and Goliath, and the Christmas story — told with warmth and without complexity. Perfect for the youngest bedtime listeners.
stories.authorBio: Anonymous compiled these Bible stories for young readers in the early twentieth century.
stories.relevance: Scripture told as story — the way it was meant to be heard at bedtime.
stories.coverScene: A kind-faced man in biblical robes sitting under a fig tree with three small children gathered around his knees, one child reaching up to touch his hand. Clay oil lamps glow on a low stone wall behind them. A star-filled sky above a flat-roofed village. Warm, gentle lamplight.

stories.title: Wee Ones' Bible Stories
stories.author: Anonymous
stories.summary: The great stories of the Bible told simply for the very youngest children.
stories.description: Bible stories retold in the simplest possible language for toddlers and very young children. The Creation, Noah's Ark, David and Goliath, and the Christmas story — told with warmth and without complexity. Perfect for the youngest bedtime listeners.
stories.authorBio: Anonymous compiled these Bible stories for young readers in the early twentieth century.
stories.relevance: Scripture told as story — the way it was meant to be heard at bedtime.
stories.coverScene: A bearded man in a long robe standing at the prow of a wooden ark on a rain-lashed sea, arms spread wide, looking up at a break in the dark clouds where a faint rainbow begins to form. Animals peer from the ark's windows — a giraffe's head, a dove on the rail. Stormy seas giving way to calm.

stories.title: Old Peter's Russian Tales
stories.author: Arthur Ransome
stories.summary: Old Peter tells his grandchildren a tale by the fire — of Baba Yaga, the firebird, and the grey wolf.
stories.description: From Old Peter's Russian Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: Arthur Ransome (1884–1967) was an English writer and journalist who lived in Russia during the Revolution. He collected these folk tales from Russian peasants and retold them as stories told by an old grandfather to his grandchildren.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.
stories.coverScene: An old man with a long white beard sitting in a carved wooden chair beside a tiled stove in a Russian cottage, two children in felt boots and embroidered shirts sitting cross-legged at his feet. A fat orange cat sleeps on the warm stove ledge. Snow piles against the frost-patterned window. Warm candlelight inside, blue winter dusk outside.

stories.title: Old Peter's Russian Tales
stories.author: Arthur Ransome
stories.summary: Old Peter tells another tale from the long Russian winter — of frost kings, enchanted animals, and the rewards of kindness.
stories.description: The second tale from Arthur Ransome's collection. Old Peter is a grandfather who tells stories by the fire to his two grandchildren, Vanya and Maroosia. The frame story is as warm as the tales themselves — a family gathered around a stove in the Russian snow.
stories.authorBio: Arthur Ransome (1884–1967) was an English author and journalist best known for his Swallows and Amazons series. Old Peter's Russian Tales, published in 1916, gathers stories he heard during his years in Russia — fairy tales of Baba Yaga, frost kings, and magical fish.
stories.relevance: Russian fairy tales have a special quality — they are wilder and more unpredictable than Western European stories, but they reward the same virtues: kindness, patience, and courage.
stories.coverScene: A girl in a red headscarf and sheepskin coat walking through a birch forest in deep snow, carrying a woven basket. Ahead of her, a small hut on chicken legs peeks from behind the silver birch trees, smoke curling from its crooked chimney. Pale winter light, long blue shadows on the snow.

stories.title: East of the Sun and West of the Moon
stories.author: Peter Christen Asbjornsen and Jorgen Moe
stories.summary: A girl rides a great white bear to a castle, loses him through curiosity, and must travel east of the sun and west of the moon to win him back.
stories.description: A poor farmer's youngest daughter agrees to go with a great white bear in exchange for her family's fortune. She lives in luxury but is warned never to light a candle at night. When curiosity gets the better of her, she discovers the bear is really an enchanted prince — and loses him. She must then journey to the end of the world to find the castle that lies east of the sun and west of the moon. One of the finest fairy tales in the Scandinavian tradition, rich with wonder and moral weight.
stories.authorBio: Peter Christen Asbjornsen (1812–1885) and Jorgen Moe (1813–1882) were Norwegian folklorists who collected and published Norwegian fairy tales, much as the Grimm brothers did for Germany.
stories.relevance: A sweeping fairy tale with a strong, determined heroine. The Norse atmosphere of trolls, enchanted castles, and northern winds makes for a mesmerizing bedtime listen.

series.name: East of the Sun and West of the Moon
series.nextTeaser: Next: The Three Billy Goats Gruff — the famous tale of the troll under the bridge.

stories.coverScene: A young girl in a simple Norse dress riding on the back of a great white bear through a snowy mountain pass at night. She grips the bear's thick fur with both hands, her cloak streaming behind her. Tall dark pines line the pass. A full moon lights the snow to silver. The bear's eyes are wise and sad.

stories.title: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz: The Cyclone
stories.author: L. Frank Baum
stories.summary: A Kansas cyclone carries Dorothy and Toto to the magical Land of Oz.
stories.description: Dorothy lives on the grey Kansas prairie with Uncle Henry and Auntie Em. When a cyclone strikes, she and Toto are carried inside the farmhouse to a strange and colorful land. Baum's opening chapter is one of the most iconic in children's literature — the grey world giving way to vivid color, and an ordinary girl stepping into an extraordinary adventure.
stories.authorBio: L. Frank Baum (1856–1919) was an American author who wrote The Wonderful Wizard of Oz in 1900, intending it as a modern American fairy tale without the frightening elements of European folklore.
stories.relevance: The opening of Oz is pure magic — short enough for a single bedtime session and ending with the perfect cliffhanger of arrival in a new world.

series.name: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz
series.nextTeaser: Next: Dorothy meets the Munchkins and the Good Witch of the North.

stories.coverScene: A small farmhouse spinning inside a dark tornado funnel, tilted at an angle, with a girl in a blue gingham dress clutching a small black dog as she peers out a window. Below, the grey Kansas prairie falls away. Above, glimpses of a strange colorful land through the swirling debris. Dark sky, one crack of lightning.

stories.title: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz: The Cowardly Lion
stories.author: L. Frank Baum
stories.summary: Dorothy and her friends meet a lion who is afraid of everything — and he joins their journey.
stories.description: Deep in the forest, a great lion leaps onto the road and tries to frighten Dorothy's companions. But when Dorothy slaps his nose and calls him a coward, the Lion breaks down in tears. He confesses he is terrified of everything, and Dorothy invites him to come to the Emerald City where the Wizard might give him courage. Baum's message — that admitting fear is the first step toward bravery — resonates with every child who has ever been afraid at bedtime.
stories.authorBio: L. Frank Baum (1856–1919) was an American author who wrote The Wonderful Wizard of Oz in 1900.
stories.relevance: The Cowardly Lion's honesty about his fears makes this the most relatable chapter for children. A natural bedtime conversation starter about being brave.

series.name: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz
series.nextTeaser: Next: The journey through the dark forest to the Emerald City.

stories.coverScene: A girl in a blue gingham dress, a scarecrow stuffed with straw, and a tin woodman standing together on a forest path, facing a large lion who sits on his haunches with tears rolling down his furry cheeks. The lion's mane is magnificent but his expression is sheepish and sorry. Dappled forest light, tall dark trees.

stories.title: The Tale of Benjamin Bunny
stories.author: Beatrix Potter
stories.summary: Peter Rabbit's cousin Benjamin Bunny convinces Peter to return to Mr. McGregor's garden to retrieve the clothes he lost — but Mr. McGregor's cat is waiting.
stories.description: From The Tale of Peter Rabbit and Others. Beatrix Potter's animal stories are marvels of compression — each one is a complete adventure in just a few pages, told in precise, witty prose with Potter's own watercolor illustrations in mind. The stories are gentle but never saccharine; Peter really could have been put in a pie.
stories.authorBio: Beatrix Potter (1866–1943) was an English writer and illustrator best known for her children's books featuring animal characters. The Tale of Peter Rabbit, first published in 1902, has sold over 45 million copies. Her stories are short, precise, and perfect for the youngest listeners.
stories.relevance: The gold standard for bedtime stories for the youngest listeners. Each tale is short enough to finish in one sitting, vivid enough to see with your eyes closed, and funny enough to make a parent smile too.
stories.coverScene: A small brown rabbit in a tam-o'-shanter cap and a slightly larger rabbit in a blue jacket creeping along the top of a garden wall, peering down at neat rows of lettuces and radishes below. A wooden garden gate stands ajar. A fat tabby cat sleeps on a sunny garden bench. English cottage garden, warm afternoon.

stories.title: The Tale of Jemima Puddle-Duck
stories.author: Beatrix Potter
stories.summary: Jemima Puddle-Duck wants to hatch her own eggs, but the charming gentleman fox she meets in the woods has other plans entirely.
stories.description: From The Tale of Peter Rabbit and Others. Beatrix Potter's animal stories are marvels of compression — each one is a complete adventure in just a few pages, told in precise, witty prose with Potter's own watercolor illustrations in mind. The stories are gentle but never saccharine; Peter really could have been put in a pie.

The Tale of Peter Rabbit, first published in 1902, has sold over 45 million copies. Her stories are short, precise, and perfect for the youngest listeners.

stories.coverScene: A white duck wearing a blue bonnet and shawl waddling up a woodland path toward a shed, looking pleased with herself. A fox in a smart coat stands in the shed doorway, smiling with too many teeth, holding the door open politely. Foxgloves and ferns along the path. Soft overcast English light.

stories.title: The Tale of Mrs. Tiggy-Winkle
stories.author: Beatrix Potter
stories.summary: Little Lucie has lost her pocket handkerchiefs and follows a path up the hill to the house of Mrs. Tiggy-Winkle, a very stout, short hedgehog who takes in washing.
stories.description: From The Tale of Peter Rabbit and Others. Beatrix Potter's animal stories are marvels of compression — each one is a complete adventure in just a few pages, told in precise, witty prose with Potter's own watercolor illustrations in mind. The stories are gentle but never saccharine; Peter really could have been put in a pie.
