stories.coverScene: A small hedgehog in an apron and cap standing at a tiny doorway built into a grassy hillside, holding out a stack of freshly pressed handkerchiefs to a little girl in a pinafore. A clothesline strung between two thorn bushes behind the hedgehog, with tiny garments fluttering. Misty Lake District hills in the distance.

stories.title: The Tale of Peter Rabbit
stories.author: Beatrix Potter
stories.summary: Naughty Peter Rabbit disobeys his mother and sneaks into Mr. McGregor's garden, where he eats too many vegetables and very nearly ends up in a pie.
stories.description: From The Tale of Peter Rabbit and Others. Beatrix Potter's animal stories are marvels of compression — each one is a complete adventure in just a few pages, told in precise, witty prose with Potter's own watercolor illustrations in mind. The stories are gentle but never saccharine; Peter really could have been put in a pie.
stories.authorBio: Beatrix Potter (1866–1943) was an English writer and illustrator best known for her children's books featuring animal characters. The Tale of Peter Rabbit, first published in 1902, has sold over 45 million copies. Her stories are short, precise, and perfect for the youngest listeners.
stories.relevance: The gold standard for bedtime stories for the youngest listeners. Each tale is short enough to finish in one sitting, vivid enough to see with your eyes closed, and funny enough to make a parent smile too.
stories.coverScene: A small brown rabbit in a blue jacket squeezing under the gate of a vegetable garden, ears flat, looking nervously over his shoulder. Rows of carrots, lettuces, and radishes behind him. A large straw hat and garden rake lean against the gate — the gardener is nearby but unseen. Warm English garden light.

stories.title: Cinderella
stories.author: Brothers Grimm
stories.summary: A girl in ashes is given a golden dress by the birds in her mother's tree — and the prince finds her at the ball.
stories.description: From the Brothers Grimm's Household Tales — the stories that European families told their children for centuries before the Grimms wrote them down. These are not the sanitized versions; they are the real tales, with real consequences and real rewards.
stories.authorBio: Jacob (1785–1863) and Wilhelm (1786–1859) Grimm were German academics and linguists who collected and published folk tales from oral tradition. Their Household Tales preserved stories that had been told in European homes for centuries — Cinderella, Snow White, Hansel and Gretel, Rapunzel — and shaped the fairy tale tradition as we know it.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told the way they were told around the fire. Short, vivid, and full of the kind of justice children understand instinctively.
stories.coverScene: A girl in a ragged dress sitting among the ashes of a kitchen hearth, looking up at a white dove that has landed on the windowsill carrying a tiny golden slipper in its beak. Pots and pans hang from iron hooks above. A broom leans against the stone wall. Warm firelight, last light of evening through the window.

stories.title: Hansel and Gretel
stories.author: Brothers Grimm
stories.summary: Two children lost in the forest find a house made of bread and sugar — and the old woman inside is not what she seems.
stories.description: From the Brothers Grimm's Household Tales — the stories that European families told their children for centuries before the Grimms wrote them down. These are not the sanitized versions; they are the real tales, with real consequences and real rewards.
stories.authorBio: Jacob (1785–1863) and Wilhelm (1786–1859) Grimm were German academics and linguists who collected and published folk tales from oral tradition. Their Household Tales preserved stories that had been told in European homes for centuries — Cinderella, Snow White, Hansel and Gretel, Rapunzel — and shaped the fairy tale tradition as we know it.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told the way they were told around the fire. Short, vivid, and full of the kind of justice children understand instinctively.
stories.coverScene: Two small children — a boy in a brown cap and a girl with braids — standing hand in hand at the edge of a dark pine forest, staring at a small cottage whose walls are made of gingerbread and whose roof is frosted with white icing. A faint curl of smoke from the chimney. Dusk light, long shadows.

stories.title: Rapunzel
stories.author: Brothers Grimm
stories.summary: A girl locked in a tower lets down her hair — and a prince climbs it.
stories.description: From the Brothers Grimm's Household Tales — the stories that European families told their children for centuries before the Grimms wrote them down. These are not the sanitized versions; they are the real tales, with real consequences and real rewards.
stories.authorBio: Jacob (1785–1863) and Wilhelm (1786–1859) Grimm were German academics and linguists who collected and published folk tales from oral tradition. Their Household Tales preserved stories that had been told in European homes for centuries — Cinderella, Snow White, Hansel and Gretel, Rapunzel — and shaped the fairy tale tradition as we know it.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told the way they were told around the fire. Short, vivid, and full of the kind of justice children understand instinctively.
stories.coverScene: A girl with impossibly long golden hair leaning from the window of a tall stone tower, her hair cascading down the tower wall like a golden waterfall. Far below, a prince on a white horse looks up with one hand shielding his eyes. Ivy grows up the tower stones. Sunset light turning the hair to fire.

stories.title: Snow White
stories.author: Brothers Grimm
stories.summary: A queen asks her mirror who is the fairest — and the mirror answers honestly.
stories.description: From the Brothers Grimm's Household Tales — the stories that European families told their children for centuries before the Grimms wrote them down. These are not the sanitized versions; they are the real tales, with real consequences and real rewards.
stories.authorBio: Jacob (1785–1863) and Wilhelm (1786–1859) Grimm were German academics and linguists who collected and published folk tales from oral tradition. Their Household Tales preserved stories that had been told in European homes for centuries — Cinderella, Snow White, Hansel and Gretel, Rapunzel — and shaped the fairy tale tradition as we know it.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told the way they were told around the fire. Short, vivid, and full of the kind of justice children understand instinctively.
stories.coverScene: A young woman with black hair and pale skin biting into a bright red apple offered by a stooped old woman in a dark hooded cloak. Behind them, a small cottage in a forest clearing with seven tiny chairs visible through the open door. Dark pines, a single shaft of light falling on the apple.

stories.title: The Adventures of Pinocchio
stories.author: Carlo Collodi
stories.summary: A carpenter finds a piece of wood that laughs and cries — and carves it into a puppet who immediately runs away.
stories.description: The real Pinocchio is wilder and stranger than the Disney version. A puppet who lies, runs from home, and gets into terrible trouble — but who keeps being given chances to become real. Collodi's original is a Catholic morality tale about the long, painful, funny process of becoming fully human.
stories.authorBio: Carlo Collodi (1826–1890) was the pen name of Carlo Lorenzini, a Florentine journalist and children's author. He wrote Pinocchio as a serial for a children's newspaper in 1881, and it became Italy's most famous children's book.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.
stories.coverScene: An old woodcarver in a leather apron sitting at a cluttered workbench, holding up a freshly carved wooden puppet with jointed limbs and a long pointed nose. The puppet's painted eyes seem to look back at him with a spark of mischief. Wood shavings curl on the floor, tools hung on the wall. Warm candlelight in the workshop.

stories.title: The Adventures of Pinocchio
stories.author: Carlo Collodi
stories.summary: Geppetto carves the puppet and names him Pinocchio — and as soon as his legs are finished, Pinocchio runs away into the street, beginning a long career of trouble.
stories.description: The second chapter of Collodi's masterpiece. The original Pinocchio is wilder, funnier, and more dangerous than any adaptation — a wooden puppet who is selfish, reckless, and constantly running from the consequences of his lies. His journey toward becoming a real boy is the original redemption story for children.
stories.authorBio: Carlo Collodi was the pen name of Carlo Lorenzini (1826–1890), an Italian author best known for The Adventures of Pinocchio. First serialized in a children's magazine in 1881, it is the story of a wooden puppet who must learn honesty, obedience, and selflessness before he can become a real boy.
stories.relevance: Pinocchio's story is fundamentally about conscience, obedience, and the hard road from selfishness to love. The puppet must learn to tell the truth, keep his promises, and put others before himself — only then does he become real.
stories.coverScene: A wooden puppet boy with a long nose running down a cobblestone street in an Italian village, arms and legs swinging stiffly, a crowd of laughing children chasing behind him. An old man in a leather apron stands in a workshop doorway, calling after the puppet with outstretched arms. Warm Mediterranean light, terracotta rooftops.

stories.title: The Heroes
stories.author: Charles Kingsley
stories.summary: Perseus sets out to slay the Gorgon Medusa — guided by Athena and armed with winged sandals and a cap of darkness.
stories.description: From The Heroes — stories about the natural world told with reverence and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Charles Kingsley (1819–1875) was an English clergyman, novelist, and social reformer who retold the Greek myths of Perseus, the Argonauts, and Theseus specifically for his own children — making them the first great English versions intended for young readers.
stories.relevance: Nature stories for bedtime — because the world outside the window is as full of stories as any fairy tale.
stories.coverScene: A young Greek hero in a short tunic and winged sandals standing at the edge of a rocky cliff above a wine-dark sea, holding a polished bronze shield that reflects the sky like a mirror. A leather satchel hangs at his side. Distant Greek islands dot the sea. Golden Aegean light, wind in his hair.

stories.title: The Water-Babies
stories.author: Charles Kingsley
stories.summary: Tom the chimney sweep falls into a river and becomes a water-baby — beginning a journey of purification and wonder.
stories.description: Kingsley's great Christian allegory for children. Tom is a chimney sweep — dirty, abused, and ignorant. He falls into a river and is transformed into a tiny water-baby, beginning a journey through underwater worlds where he must learn to be clean inside and out. It is a baptismal story, a nature story, and a social protest rolled into one strange, beautiful book.
stories.authorBio: Charles Kingsley (1819–1875) was an English clergyman, novelist, and social reformer. He wrote The Water-Babies in 1863 as both a fairy tale for his youngest son and a protest against child labor.
stories.relevance: A journey story for bedtime — transformation, wonder, and the idea that everyone deserves to be made clean.
stories.coverScene: A small, soot-covered boy chimney sweep peering down from inside a dark chimney flue at a clean white bedroom below, where a little girl sleeps peacefully in a lace-trimmed bed. His face is black with soot except for two wide, wondering eyes. Moonlight streams through the bedroom window.

stories.title: Cinderella
stories.author: Charles Perrault
stories.summary: A girl in cinders is given one night of beauty by her fairy godmother — and leaves behind a glass slipper.
stories.description: The original Cinderella, as Perrault told it in 1697. Not the Disney version — gentler in some ways, sharper in others. The pumpkin coach, the glass slipper, and the stroke of midnight are all here, but so is Perrault's dry wit and his Catholic sense that goodness is its own reward.
stories.authorBio: Charles Perrault (1628–1703) was a French author and member of the Académie française who established the modern fairy tale genre. A devout Catholic, he published Tales of Mother Goose in 1697, giving literary form to Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Puss in Boots, and Little Red Riding Hood.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told by a devout Catholic Frenchman in 1697. These are the versions that shaped every retelling since.
stories.coverScene: A girl in a shimmering ball gown racing down a grand marble staircase at midnight, one glass slipper left behind on the top step. Her coach — a golden pumpkin on wheels — waits at the bottom with white mice-horses. A clock tower behind the palace strikes twelve. Moonlight and magic sparkles.

stories.title: Puss in Boots
stories.author: Charles Perrault
stories.summary: A clever cat inherits nothing but his wits and talks his way to a kingdom for his master.
stories.description: The most delightfully amoral of Perrault's tales — a cat who lies, cheats, and bluffs his way to a fortune. Children love it for the cleverness; parents can use it to talk about when cunning is and isn't a virtue.
stories.authorBio: Charles Perrault (1628–1703) was a French author and member of the Académie française who established the modern fairy tale genre. A devout Catholic, he published Tales of Mother Goose in 1697, giving literary form to Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Puss in Boots, and Little Red Riding Hood.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told by a devout Catholic Frenchman in 1697. These are the versions that shaped every retelling since.
stories.coverScene: A ginger cat wearing tall leather boots, a plumed hat, and a sword belt, striding confidently down a country road with one paw raised in a theatrical gesture. Behind him, a young man in peasant clothes watches with amazement. A castle with turrets rises on a distant hill. Bright morning light, road dust.

stories.title: Sleeping Beauty
stories.author: Charles Perrault
stories.summary: A princess pricks her finger on a spindle and sleeps for a hundred years, waiting for the prince brave enough to wake her.
stories.description: Perrault's Sleeping Beauty is longer and stranger than the version most people know. The hundred-year sleep, the enchanted castle, and the prince who arrives to find roses growing over the walls — it is one of the great images of patience and hope in fairy tale literature.
stories.authorBio: Charles Perrault (1628–1703) was a French author and member of the Académie française who established the modern fairy tale genre. A devout Catholic, he published Tales of Mother Goose in 1697, giving literary form to Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Puss in Boots, and Little Red Riding Hood.
stories.relevance: The original fairy tales, told by a devout Catholic Frenchman in 1697. These are the versions that shaped every retelling since.
stories.coverScene: A princess in a pale gown lying asleep on a canopied bed in a tower room, one hand trailing off the edge. Roses have grown through the window and twine around the bedposts and canopy frame. A spinning wheel lies overturned in the corner. Dust motes hang in a single beam of golden light piercing the thorny vines.

stories.title: Sing-Song: A Nursery Rhyme Book
stories.author: Christina Rossetti
stories.summary: Gentle nursery rhymes by Christina Rossetti — about stars, babies, kittens, and the coming of sleep.
stories.description: Christina Rossetti's Sing-Song is a collection of 126 nursery rhymes written with a poet's ear and a Christian devotional sensibility. They are about simple things — wind, rain, babies, animals, sleep — and they carry the same quiet depth as her adult poetry. Perfect for the very youngest listeners.
stories.authorBio: Christina Rossetti (1830–1894) was an English poet and devout Anglican whose work includes Goblin Market, In the Bleak Midwinter, and this collection of nursery rhymes. She wrote with precision, beauty, and a deep religious faith.
stories.relevance: Nursery rhymes by a great poet — the kind of thing that sounds simple and stays in memory forever.
stories.coverScene: A mother and a small child sitting together in a window seat, the mother holding an open book of verses on her lap while the child points at something outside — a robin on a snow-covered branch. Lace curtains, a vase of dried flowers on the sill. Soft winter light, cozy interior warmth.

stories.title: Sing-Song: A Nursery Rhyme Book
stories.author: Christina Rossetti
stories.summary: More of Rossetti's tender nursery verses — about kittens, caterpillars, the wind, the moon, and the gentle rhythm of a child's day from morning to bedtime.
stories.description: The second selection from Christina Rossetti's Sing-Song. These are poems written with a mother's tenderness and a poet's ear — short, musical, and full of the natural world. They are among the finest nursery rhymes in the English language.
stories.authorBio: Christina Rossetti (1830–1894) was an English poet associated with the Pre-Raphaelite movement. A devout Anglican, her Sing-Song: A Nursery Rhyme Book is a collection of tender, musical verses for the very young — about lambs, babies, wind, and sleep.
stories.relevance: Perfect lullaby-length poems for the youngest listeners. Rossetti's verses have the same soothing, rhythmic quality as a rocking chair — gentle repetition, soft images, natural cadence.
stories.coverScene: A small child lying on her stomach in a garden, chin on hands, watching a ladybug crawl along a daisy stem. A tabby cat naps in the sun beside her. Hollyhocks and sweet peas climb a wooden fence behind them. Drowsy midsummer afternoon light, bees drifting among the flowers.

stories.title: A Christmas Carol: Marley's Ghost
stories.author: Charles Dickens
stories.summary: The ghost of Jacob Marley visits his old partner Scrooge on Christmas Eve, dragging chains of greed.
stories.description: Ebenezer Scrooge is the meanest man in London. On Christmas Eve, his dead partner Jacob Marley appears as a ghost, bound in heavy chains forged from a lifetime of selfishness. Marley warns Scrooge that three spirits will visit him — and that this is his last chance to change. Dickens wrote this in just six weeks in 1843, and it became the most famous Christmas story ever told.
stories.authorBio: Charles Dickens (1812–1870) was an English novelist whose works include Oliver Twist, David Copperfield, and Great Expectations. A Christmas Carol was written partly as social commentary on poverty in Victorian England.
stories.relevance: A timeless Christmas story about redemption. Best suited for older children who can handle the ghostly atmosphere — the payoff is one of the most joyful endings in literature.

series.name: A Christmas Carol
series.nextTeaser: Next: The Ghost of Christmas Past takes Scrooge on a journey through his memories.

stories.coverScene: A thin, hunched old man in a nightcap and dressing gown holding a candle, staring in horror at the ghostly figure of a man wrapped in heavy chains and padlocked cashboxes who floats through the closed door of a cold, dark counting house. The ghost's jaw is bound with a cloth. Frost on the windowpanes, a meager fire nearly dead in the grate.

stories.title: Five Children and It
stories.author: E. Nesbit
stories.summary: Five children dig in a gravel pit and find a Psammead — a sand-fairy who grants wishes that expire at sunset.
stories.description: The first chapter of one of the great children's fantasies. Five siblings move to a house in the country, go exploring, and dig up a furry, bat-eared creature who has been sleeping in the sand since the dinosaurs. He grants one wish per day, each of which goes spectacularly wrong. Nesbit invented the template for every 'be careful what you wish for' children's story that followed.
stories.authorBio: E. Nesbit (1858–1924) was an English author and co-founder of the Fabian Society who wrote some of the most inventive children's novels of her era. Five Children and It, The Railway Children, and The Enchanted Castle blend everyday family life with magic in a way that felt entirely new and still feels fresh.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.
stories.coverScene: Five children in Edwardian clothes — three boys and two girls — digging in a sandy gravel pit and discovering a round, furry creature with bat-like ears, a tubby body, and eyes on stalks like a snail. The creature blinks up at them crossly from the sand. Bright English summer day, chalk downs in the distance.

stories.title: Five Children and It
stories.author: E. Nesbit
stories.summary: The children make their first wish — to be 'as beautiful as the day' — and discover that wishes granted by a sand-fairy don't work out quite the way you expect.
stories.description: The second chapter of E. Nesbit's comic masterpiece. The Psammead grants one wish per day, and each wish goes spectacularly wrong. It is the template for every 'be careful what you wish for' story that came after.
stories.authorBio: E. Nesbit (1858–1924) was an English author and co-founder of the Fabian Society who wrote some of the most inventive children's novels of her era. Five Children and It, The Railway Children, and The Enchanted Castle blend everyday family life with magic in a way that felt entirely new and still feels fresh.
stories.relevance: A funny, cautionary story about getting exactly what you asked for. Each chapter's wish is a self-contained comic disaster — perfect bedtime episodes.
stories.coverScene: Five children in Edwardian clothes standing in a circle around a furry, bat-eared creature perched on a mound of sand, their faces lit with excitement and disbelief as the creature's eyes extend on their stalks and it puffs itself up importantly. An English meadow stretches behind them under a wide blue sky.

stories.title: The Railway Children
stories.author: E. Nesbit
stories.summary: Three children — Roberta, Peter, and Phyllis — move from London to a cottage near a railway line when their father mysteriously goes away. The railway becomes their world, and the trains carry more than passengers.
stories.description: E. Nesbit's most beloved realistic novel. The Railway Children is about a family dealing with loss and uncertainty while maintaining courage, humor, and kindness. The children's adventures on the railway — waving to trains, preventing disasters, befriending the station porter — are both exciting and deeply moving.
stories.authorBio: E. Nesbit (1858–1924) was an English author and co-founder of the Fabian Society who wrote some of the most inventive children's novels of her era. Five Children and It, The Railway Children, and The Enchanted Castle blend everyday family life with magic in a way that felt entirely new and still feels fresh.
stories.relevance: A story about courage, loyalty, and the strength of family in difficult times. Roberta's quiet determination to hold her family together is one of the most moving portrayals of childhood responsibility in children's literature.
stories.coverScene: Three children — two girls and a boy in Edwardian clothes — standing on a railway platform at a small country station, their mother behind them looking tired but brave, surrounded by trunks and hat boxes. The eldest girl holds her younger siblings' hands. A steam train disappears around a bend. Grey morning, a new and uncertain beginning.

stories.title: Nonsense Verses
stories.author: Edward Lear
stories.summary: The Owl and the Pussycat, the Jumblies, the Dong with the Luminous Nose, and other gloriously absurd poems from the master of English nonsense.
stories.description: Edward Lear's nonsense poetry is pure delight — musical, absurd, and irresistible to children. His poems create entire worlds out of invented words, impossible creatures, and journeys to nowhere. They are the English language at play.
stories.authorBio: Edward Lear (1812–1888) was an English artist and poet known for his literary nonsense, especially his limericks and poems like 'The Owl and the Pussycat.' He brought sheer delight and absurdity to children's verse, and his work has been in continuous print for over 150 years.
stories.relevance: Pure fun. Lear's poems are the antidote to overly earnest children's literature — they exist to make children laugh, to delight in the sound of words, and to prove that nonsense is a perfectly serious business.
stories.coverScene: An owl and a pussycat sailing in a beautiful pea-green boat on a moonlit sea, the owl playing a small guitar while the cat gazes at the moon. A jar of honey and a five-pound note sit in the bow. Stars reflected in the calm water. Whimsical, dreamy moonlight.

stories.title: Jataka Tales
stories.author: Ellen C. Babbitt
stories.summary: A monkey king sacrifices himself to save his troop — one of the ancient Jataka tales of wisdom and virtue.
stories.description: From Jataka Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: Ellen C. Babbitt (1872–?) was an American educator who retold the Buddhist Jataka birth stories for children in 1912. Each tale has a clear moral, and the stories feature animals as the Buddha's past lives — very similar in structure to Aesop's fables.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.
stories.coverScene: A wise monkey sitting cross-legged on a river rock, one paw raised in a teaching gesture, speaking to a circle of forest animals — a deer, a crane, a rabbit, and a turtle — gathered on the bank below. Tropical trees with broad leaves arch overhead. A river flows past with lily pads. Warm, dappled jungle light.

stories.title: Jataka Tales
stories.author: Ellen C. Babbitt
stories.summary: Another animal fable from ancient India — teaching cleverness, generosity, and the wisdom of thinking before you act.
stories.description: The second tale from Ellen C. Babbitt's retelling of the Jataka Tales. These ancient Buddhist stories — over 2,000 years old — use animal characters to teach moral lessons that are universal: be kind, be clever, think of others, and don't be fooled by flattery.
stories.authorBio: Ellen C. Babbitt (1878–1946) was an American author who retold the Jataka Tales — ancient Buddhist stories about the previous lives of the Buddha — for young Western readers. The tales teach kindness, cleverness, and generosity through animal fables.
stories.relevance: Some of the oldest moral tales in the world, retold for young readers. The Jataka Tales predate Aesop and share the same teaching-through-story tradition.
stories.coverScene: A small white elephant kneeling beside a lotus pond in a forest clearing, offering a lotus blossom to an old man in simple robes who sits beneath a banyan tree. Other animals watch respectfully from the edge of the clearing. Golden afternoon light filtering through enormous banyan leaves.

stories.title: Black Folk Tales
stories.author: Erick Berry
stories.summary: Hausa folk tales from Nigeria — short, clever stories of animals and wisdom.
stories.description: From Black Folk Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: Erick Berry (pen name of Evangeline Champlin, 1892–1974) lived in West Africa in the 1920s and retold Hausa folk tales from Nigeria. Short, engaging stories with animal characters and moral outcomes.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.
stories.coverScene: A spider the size of a man's hand sitting at the center of a magnificent golden web stretched between two baobab trees, telling a story to a circle of animals gathered below — a leopard, an elephant, a tortoise, and a hornbill. West African savanna at sunset, the sky blazing orange and red behind the silhouetted baobabs.

stories.title: The Biography of a Grizzly
stories.author: Ernest Thompson Seton
stories.summary: Wahb the grizzly bear loses his mother and brothers on the first page — and spends a lifetime growing into the king of the mountains.
stories.description: From The Biography of a Grizzly — stories about the natural world told with reverence and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Ernest Thompson Seton tells the life story of Wahb, a grizzly bear in the Yellowstone country — from orphaned cub to the greatest bear in the mountains.
stories.relevance: Nature stories for bedtime — because the world outside the window is as full of stories as any fairy tale.
stories.coverScene: A small grizzly bear cub sitting upright in a mountain meadow full of wildflowers, tilting its head curiously at a butterfly that has landed on its nose. The cub's mother — a massive grizzly — grazes in the background near a stand of pines. Snow-capped peaks behind them. Clear Rocky Mountain morning light.

stories.title: Wild Animals I Have Known
stories.author: Ernest Thompson Seton
stories.summary: Lobo, the king of the Currumpaw wolves, outsmarts every trap — until the hunters find what he loves.
stories.description: From Wild Animals I Have Known — stories about the natural world told with reverence and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Ernest Thompson Seton (1860–1946) was a British-Canadian naturalist and author who helped found the Boy Scouts of America. His animal stories treat their subjects as individuals with dignity and courage.
stories.relevance: Nature stories for bedtime — because the world outside the window is as full of stories as any fairy tale.
stories.coverScene: A large grey wolf standing on a rocky ridge above a western canyon, head raised in a howl, silhouetted against a vast sky streaked with sunset colors. Below the ridge, a cattle ranch spreads across the valley — tiny buildings, fences, and a winding river. The wolf's posture is proud and wild.

stories.title: Bannertail: The Story of a Graysquirrel
stories.author: Ernest Thompson Seton
stories.summary: A young gray squirrel, orphaned and raised by a kind human, must learn to survive in the wild — finding food, avoiding hawks and cats, and discovering the ancient ways of his kind.
stories.description: Seton's most charming animal biography. Bannertail is a real character — curious, brave, and resourceful — and his adventures in the woods are based on Seton's own careful observations of gray squirrels over many years.
stories.authorBio: Ernest Thompson Seton (1860–1946) was a British-born Canadian-American naturalist, author, and illustrator who co-founded the Boy Scouts of America. His animal stories — drawn from real observation — combine scientific accuracy with narrative power. He believed that children who understood wild animals would grow into better people.
stories.relevance: A nature story grounded in real observation. Seton makes the reader care about a squirrel the way they'd care about a person — because he understood that every wild animal has a story.
stories.coverScene: A small grey squirrel with a conspicuously bushy tail sitting on a high oak branch, clutching an acorn and peering down at a red-tailed hawk circling below. Autumn leaves in gold and copper surround the squirrel. A vast forest stretches to the horizon. Crisp fall light, blue sky.

stories.title: Titus: A Comrade of the Cross
stories.author: Florence Morse Kingsley
stories.summary: A young Roman encounters the early Christians — and discovers a faith that changes everything.
stories.description: Florence Morse Kingsley's vivid historical fiction set in the time of the early Church. Titus is a young Roman who encounters the followers of Christ and finds himself drawn into a community of courage, sacrifice, and radical love. For older children ready for stories about what it cost to be a Christian when being a Christian could cost your life.
stories.authorBio: Florence Morse Kingsley (1859–1937) was an American author who wrote a series of popular historical novels about the early Church. Titus, Stephen, and Paul follow characters through the first century of Christianity.
stories.relevance: Historical fiction with a Christian spine — for the child who is ready to hear what faith looked like when it was dangerous.

series.name: Titus: A Comrade of the Cross

stories.coverScene: A young Roman man in a simple tunic and sandals standing at the edge of a dusty road outside Jerusalem, shielding his eyes against the sun as he looks toward the walled city on the hill. Three crosses stand dark on a distant ridge. A few pilgrims walk the road. Late afternoon light, solemn and golden.

stories.title: A Little Princess
stories.author: Frances Hodgson Burnett
stories.summary: Sara Crewe arrives at Miss Minchin's boarding school as the richest pupil — and imagines herself a princess.
stories.description: The first chapter of Burnett's great novel about imagination and dignity. Sara Crewe has everything — and then loses everything — and discovers that being a princess is not about what you have but about how you behave when you have nothing. One of the great stories about grace under pressure.
stories.authorBio: Frances Hodgson Burnett (1849–1924) was an English-American novelist who also wrote A Little Princess and Little Lord Fauntleroy. The moors and walled gardens of her childhood Manchester thread through The Secret Garden, written in 1911.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.
stories.coverScene: A small girl with dark curly hair in a green velvet coat stepping out of a hansom cab on a rainy London street, holding her father's hand. A grey stone building — a girls' seminary — looms behind iron gates. Gaslights glow yellow in the drizzle. Her expression is brave but her eyes are wide. Victorian London, grey and wet.

stories.title: The Secret Garden
stories.author: Frances Hodgson Burnett
stories.summary: Mary Lennox, sour and lonely, is sent from India to live with her uncle on the Yorkshire moors.
stories.description: Chapter 1 — 'There Is No One Left.' A cholera outbreak in colonial India leaves nine-year-old Mary Lennox an orphan, alone in a great empty bungalow until soldiers come and find her hiding behind a curtain. The chapter is quiet and a little sad and very precise about her loneliness. She is dispatched across the world to a great, draughty house on the Yorkshire moors belonging to a hunchbacked uncle she has never met — and the rest of the book begins from there. One of the great quiet openings in children's literature.
stories.authorBio: Frances Hodgson Burnett (1849–1924) was an English-American novelist who also wrote A Little Princess and Little Lord Fauntleroy. She moved between England and the United States all her life, and the moors and walled gardens of her childhood Manchester thread through The Secret Garden, written in 1911.
stories.relevance: A long, slow, healing book — about gardens and weather and a child who is allowed to be cross. Pairs well with rainy nights and tea.

series.name: The Secret Garden
series.nextTeaser: Mary arrives at Misselthwaite Manor and meets the brisk Mrs Medlock — and on her first morning, hears a strange sound she's been told is only the wind.

stories.coverScene: A thin, sour-faced girl in a black mourning dress standing alone in the corridor of a vast, cold English manor house, one hand on a heavy door, listening. Dust motes float in a shaft of grey light from a high window. Closed doors stretch away in both directions. Empty, echoing, and forbidding.

stories.title: The Secret Garden
stories.author: Frances Hodgson Burnett
stories.summary: Mary meets Martha, the Yorkshire maid who tells her about the locked garden, the crying in the night, and the moor where everything grows wild and free.
stories.description: Frances Hodgson Burnett's most enduring novel — the story of a sour, lonely girl who discovers a hidden garden and, in bringing it back to life, brings herself and others back to life too. The Secret Garden is about the healing power of nature, friendship, and the stubborn persistence of growing things.
stories.authorBio: Frances Hodgson Burnett (1849–1924) was an English-American novelist who also wrote A Little Princess and Little Lord Fauntleroy. The moors and walled gardens of her childhood Manchester thread through The Secret Garden, written in 1911.
stories.relevance: A slow, beautiful story about transformation — Mary's character unfolds as gradually as the garden itself. Each chapter reveals a little more of the mystery and a little more of Mary's thawing heart.
stories.coverScene: A girl in a grey coat and bonnet standing on the Yorkshire moors in a bitter wind, staring at a huge, dark manor house half-hidden in mist across the heather. A servant in a shawl stands behind her with a lantern. Purple-grey moorland stretches to the horizon under heavy clouds.

stories.title: The Secret Garden
stories.author: Frances Hodgson Burnett
stories.summary: Mary begins to explore the grounds and meets Ben Weatherstaff, the old gardener, and a friendly robin who seems to want to show her something hidden behind the ivy.
stories.description: Frances Hodgson Burnett's most enduring novel — the story of a sour, lonely girl who discovers a hidden garden and, in bringing it back to life, brings herself and others back to life too. The Secret Garden is about the healing power of nature, friendship, and the stubborn persistence of growing things.
stories.authorBio: Frances Hodgson Burnett (1849–1924) was an English-American novelist who also wrote A Little Princess and Little Lord Fauntleroy. The moors and walled gardens of her childhood Manchester thread through The Secret Garden, written in 1911.
stories.relevance: A slow, beautiful story about transformation — Mary's character unfolds as gradually as the garden itself. Each chapter reveals a little more of the mystery and a little more of Mary's thawing heart.
stories.coverScene: A girl kneeling on the ground beside a high ivy-covered wall, brushing away dead leaves to reveal green shoots pushing through the dark earth. A robin perches on the wall above her, watching with its head tilted. Beyond the wall, bare branches of climbing roses hint at a garden long abandoned. Pale early-spring light.

stories.title: Baby
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Tender poems about the wonder of a new baby — written for the youngest listeners.
stories.description: A short poem by George MacDonald, the great Scottish Christian fantasist. MacDonald's poems for children have the same luminous quality as his fairy tales — simple on the surface, deep underneath.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: Short enough for the smallest children, beautiful enough for the parent reading aloud. MacDonald's poems are lullabies in everything but name.
stories.coverScene: A sleeping infant in a carved wooden cradle by a cottage window, moonlight falling across the blanket. A single candle burns on the windowsill. Outside, a garden of white roses glows under the moon. The room is simple — stone walls, a spinning wheel in the corner. Utterly peaceful, silver and blue light.

stories.title: Bedtime
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: A gentle poem about the coming of sleep and the peace of the night.
stories.description: A short poem by George MacDonald, the great Scottish Christian fantasist. MacDonald's poems for children have the same luminous quality as his fairy tales — simple on the surface, deep underneath.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: Short enough for the smallest children, beautiful enough for the parent reading aloud. MacDonald's poems are lullabies in everything but name.
stories.coverScene: A small child in a white nightgown climbing into a high four-poster bed, one foot on a wooden step stool, arms reaching for the pillow. A grandmother in a shawl sits nearby in a rocking chair with a book. A single candle on the nightstand. Stars visible through the window. Warm amber candlelight.

stories.title: The Day Boy and the Night Girl
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: A wicked witch raises two children in opposite conditions — one who has never seen the night, and one who has never seen the day. When they finally meet, everything changes.
stories.description: Also known as Photogen and Nycteris. A boy raised only in sunlight and a girl raised only in moonlight must discover each other — and the full world — to break the witch's power. It is one of MacDonald's most beautiful shorter tales, about complementarity and wholeness.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A story about how no one is complete alone — the day boy needs the night girl, and she needs him. MacDonald weaves theology into fairy tale so gently that children absorb it as wonder.
stories.coverScene: Two children meeting at the threshold of a cave at twilight — a boy in sun-bleached clothes stepping out of golden afternoon light, and a girl in a silver-white dress emerging from soft moonlit darkness inside the cave. Their hands almost touch across the boundary of light and shadow. A landscape half in day, half in night.

stories.title: The Gifts of the Child Christ
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: On Christmas night, a neglected little girl named Sophy receives a gift that no one else can see — and through it, begins to mend her broken family.
stories.description: MacDonald's most explicitly Christian short story. A cold household is warmed by the faith of a small child and the mysterious workings of grace at Christmas. It is tender without being sentimental, and its vision of Christmas cuts deeper than any Dickens.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A Christmas story in the deepest sense — about what the coming of the Christ Child actually means for a family. Perfect for Advent and Christmas bedtime listening.
stories.coverScene: A small girl in a white dress standing before a wooden door in a stone wall, holding up a simple wildflower bouquet. The door is ajar, and warm golden light spills through the crack. Snow lies on the ground around her bare feet. A single star burns brightly directly overhead. Christmas Eve stillness.

stories.title: The Light Princess
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: A princess cursed to have no gravity floats through life laughing — but she has lost the ability to cry.
stories.description: A wicked aunt curses a newborn princess to have no gravity — she floats to the ceiling, bounces off walls, and laughs at everything because she cannot take anything seriously. It sounds funny, and it is, but MacDonald is also writing about what it means to have no weight in the world — no sorrow, no depth, no love that costs anything. The cure, when it comes, costs everything.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: Funny enough for young children, deep enough for parents to think about afterward. MacDonald at his most playful and his most serious at the same time.
stories.coverScene: A baby princess floating near the ceiling of a grand castle nursery, her christening gown billowing around her, laughing as the nursemaids below reach up with outstretched arms trying to catch her. The king and queen watch from the doorway with expressions of bewildered delight. Tall arched windows, candles in sconces, everything light and airy.

stories.title: The Light Princess
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: The princess discovers the lake, the one place where she feels weight, and a prince discovers her there.
stories.description: A wicked aunt curses a newborn princess to have no gravity — she floats to the ceiling, bounces off walls, and laughs at everything because she cannot take anything seriously. It sounds funny, and it is, but MacDonald is also writing about what it means to have no weight in the world — no sorrow, no depth, no love that costs anything. The cure, when it comes, costs everything.

stories.coverScene: A princess in a flowing dress floating above the surface of a moonlit lake, trailing one hand in the water and laughing, while a young prince in a rowboat reaches up toward her with both hands. Water lilies dot the lake surface. The castle reflected in the still water. Silver moonlight on everything.

stories.title: The Light Princess and Other Fairy Tales
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: A collection of MacDonald's shorter fairy tales, beginning with the story of a princess cursed to have no gravity — she floats through life laughing, unable to take anything seriously, until love teaches her weight.
stories.description: This collection gathers several of MacDonald's shorter fairy tales into one volume. The stories share MacDonald's signature blend of playfulness and depth — princesses, enchantments, and moral transformation told with a poet's ear and a minister's heart.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A sampler of MacDonald's fairy-tale genius. The stories are self-contained and varied enough to sustain many bedtimes.
stories.coverScene: A collection of fairy-tale vignettes: a castle floating in clouds, a princess walking on air, a giant sunflower growing through a cottage roof, and a child talking to a shadow on the wall — all arranged as if they were illustrations peeking from the pages of an open book. Warm, dreamy, candlelit atmosphere.

stories.title: The Lost Princess
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Two girls — a spoiled princess named Rosamond and a wild shepherd's daughter named Agnes — are both lost in a great forest, where a wise woman begins the hard work of teaching them who they really are.
stories.description: Also known as A Double Story or The Wise Woman's Telling. Two girls from opposite ends of society are equally in need of correction — one spoiled by privilege, the other by neglect. MacDonald's story is about the painful, necessary work of becoming good, told through the language of fairy tale.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A fairy tale about character formation — the kind of slow, honest growth that MacDonald believed was the real adventure. Perfect for children old enough to understand that being lost can be the beginning of being found.
stories.coverScene: A princess in a fine dress sitting on the floor of a forest clearing, mud on her hem, looking cross and confused. Opposite her, a peasant girl in patched clothes sits just as crossly. Between them, a wise old woman in a grey cloak watches both girls with patient, knowing eyes. Deep forest, filtered green light.

stories.title: At the Back of the North Wind
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Little Diamond meets the North Wind — a beautiful, terrible woman who carries him through the night sky.
stories.description: A coachman's son named Diamond lives in a hayloft above the stable. One night the North Wind comes through a crack in the wall and takes him flying over London. She is beautiful and frightening and kind, and Diamond comes to love her. This is MacDonald's most mystical children's book — a story about death and heaven told so gently that children hear it as a fairy tale and adults hear it as theology.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: One of the great bedtime books — literally about a child being carried through the night. The prose has a dreamy, lullaby quality that suits the hour.

series.name: At the Back of the North Wind
series.nextTeaser: Diamond rides with North Wind across London and begins to understand that she is not always gentle.

stories.coverScene: A small boy in a nightshirt sitting up in a hayloft bed, his hair blown wild, staring in wonder at a beautiful woman made of wind and starlight who floats in the open loft door. Her hair streams behind her like a comet's tail. The hay swirls around her. Dark night sky full of stars beyond the stable door.

stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Diamond rides with North Wind across London and begins to understand that she is not always gentle.

series.name: At the Back of the North Wind

stories.coverScene: A small boy riding on the back of a beautiful woman made of wind, high above a moonlit city of chimneys and church steeples, her hair and cloak streaming behind them like a banner of starlight. Below, the city is tiny — rooftops, lampposts, a winding river. The boy clings to her with trust. Cold clear night sky.

stories.title: The Princess and Curdie
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Curdie, now a young miner, is losing his sense of wonder — until the great-great-grandmother summons him for a new mission, and gives him a terrifying gift: the ability to feel the true nature of anyone's hand.
stories.description: The sequel to The Princess and the Goblin. Curdie is older, harder, beginning to doubt his childhood experiences. The grandmother gives him a quest to save the king and the gift of discernment — when he shakes someone's hand, he feels the animal they are becoming. It is MacDonald's darkest and most profound children's book.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A story about growing up without growing cynical. The gift of the grandmother's fire — the ability to see through surfaces to the truth of a person — is one of the great images in children's literature.
stories.coverScene: A boy in miner's clothes — leather cap, rough trousers, a pickaxe over his shoulder — walking through a dark mine tunnel toward a distant glow. His hands are strong and work-worn. Crystals glint in the rock walls around him. Ahead, a faint golden light suggests something magical beyond the next turn.

stories.title: The Princess and the Goblin
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Princess Irene discovers a mysterious staircase in her mountain home and meets her beautiful great-great-grandmother.
stories.description: George MacDonald's most beloved children's story — a tale of a princess, a miner's boy, and the goblins beneath the mountain. It is a book about courage and trust, about following a thread you cannot see, and about the kind of grandmother who lives at the top of a tower and spins moonlight. MacDonald wrote it for children but meant it for everyone. C.S. Lewis called it a book that had 'baptised his imagination.'
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A slow, luminous fairy tale that rewards being read chapter by chapter at bedtime. The grandmother's thread is one of the great images in children's literature — a child following something she trusts through the dark.

series.name: The Princess and the Goblin
series.nextTeaser: Curdie the miner's boy hears the goblins singing beneath the mountain — and their words are not kind.

stories.coverScene: A small princess with dark curly hair in a white dress standing at the bottom of a narrow tower staircase, holding a candle, looking up into the spiraling darkness above. Cobwebs hang from the stone walls. A faint golden light glows from somewhere far above, as if someone is spinning up there. The girl's face shows curiosity, not fear.

stories.title: The Princess and the Goblin
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Curdie the miner's boy discovers the goblins' tunnels and their terrible plan against the princess.
stories.description: George MacDonald's most beloved children's story — a tale of a princess, a miner's boy, and the goblins beneath the mountain. It is a book about courage and trust, about following a thread you cannot see, and about the kind of grandmother who lives at the top of a tower and spins moonlight. series.name: The Princess and the Goblin
series.nextTeaser: Irene tries to show Curdie the thread her grandmother gave her, but he cannot see it.

stories.coverScene: A small princess following a shining silver thread through a dark castle corridor, the thread leading from her finger around corners and up staircases. At the end of the corridor, a doorway glows with warm golden light. The princess walks confidently despite the darkness. Stone walls, tapestries, guttering torches.

stories.title: The Princess and the Goblin
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Irene follows her grandmother's thread through the dark and the goblins close in on the castle.
stories.description: George MacDonald's most beloved children's story — a tale of a princess, a miner's boy, and the goblins beneath the mountain. It is a book about courage and trust, about following a thread you cannot see, and about the kind of grandmother who lives at the top of a tower and spins moonlight. MacDonald wrote it for children but meant it for everyone. C.S. Lewis called it a book that had 'baptised his imagination.'

stories.coverScene: A boy with a miner's lamp crouching in a low tunnel deep underground, peering through a crack in the rock at a cavern full of misshapen goblins — bulbous heads, long arms, no toes — hammering and chanting around a stone table. The boy's face is lit by the greenish glow of the cavern. Dark, eerie, but more strange than frightening.

stories.title: Ranald Bannerman's Boyhood
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: Ranald Bannerman looks back on his childhood in the Scottish Highlands — the moors, the games, the friendships, and the quiet faith of his father the minister.
stories.description: MacDonald's most autobiographical novel, told as the memoirs of a minister's son growing up in rural Scotland. It is gentle, funny, and deeply moral — a picture of childhood shaped by nature, community, and Christian faith.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A warm, episodic story perfect for bedtime — each chapter is a self-contained adventure from a Highland boyhood. It shows what MacDonald believed childhood should be: free, adventurous, and rooted in faith.
stories.coverScene: A Scottish boy in a kilt and rough jacket sitting on a stone wall overlooking a Highland glen, a collie dog at his side, watching the sun set behind purple mountains. A small stone cottage with a peat-smoke chimney sits below in the valley. Heather covers the hillside. Warm amber light on the boy's face.

stories.title: The Shadows
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: An old man attends a parliament of shadows and learns that shadows are not frightening — they are servants of the light, doing quiet, necessary work in the world.
stories.description: A strange, beautiful short story in which an old man discovers that shadows have their own society, their own parliament, their own mission. MacDonald uses the conceit to explore how darkness and light work together — how the shadow cast by truth can be a comfort, not a threat.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: Perfect for older children who are beginning to think about fear and darkness. MacDonald's shadows are gentle, purposeful, and ultimately reassuring — they exist because the light exists.
stories.coverScene: A man lying in bed watching shadows dance on the wall — but these shadows have lives of their own: one shadow bows gracefully, another holds out a shadow hand, a third peels away from the wall and floats across the room like a dark leaf. Candlelight flickers on the bedroom wall. The man watches with wonder, not fear. Blue-grey night tones.

stories.title: The Wind and the Moon
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: The wind and the moon argue over who can blow out the other — a playful poem about pride and persistence.
stories.description: A short poem by George MacDonald, the great Scottish Christian fantasist. MacDonald's poems for children have the same luminous quality as his fairy tales — simple on the surface, deep underneath.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: Short enough for the smallest children, beautiful enough for the parent reading aloud. MacDonald's poems are lullabies in everything but name.
stories.coverScene: The wind — depicted as a wild-haired figure in a billowing cloak — blowing fiercely at a round, smiling moon who hangs serenely in the sky, unmoved. Trees bend, clouds race, leaves fly, but the moon just smiles. A landscape below of rolling hills and a single church steeple. The moon's glow is calm and golden, the wind's cloak is dark blue.

stories.title: The Wise Woman
stories.author: George MacDonald
stories.summary: A mysterious wise woman appears to set right a kingdom gone wrong — beginning with a princess who has never been told 'no' and a shepherd's daughter who has never been shown love.
stories.description: MacDonald's most direct fairy tale about moral education. The Wise Woman is fierce, loving, and uncompromising — she knows that real kindness sometimes looks like severity. The story is MacDonald at his most allegorical and most powerful.
stories.authorBio: George MacDonald (1824–1905) was a Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister whose fairy tales and fantasies directly inspired C.S. Lewis, J.R.R. Tolkien, and G.K. Chesterton. Lewis called MacDonald's Phantastes the book that 'baptised my imagination.' His children's stories carry a quiet, persistent faith — not preachy, but luminous.
stories.relevance: A story about what it truly means to grow up — not in years, but in character. The Wise Woman's methods are strange and sometimes frightening, but her love is never in doubt.
stories.coverScene: A wise old woman in a grey hooded cloak standing at the door of a cottage buried in the roots of a massive oak tree, looking calmly at a spoiled little girl who stands before her with clenched fists and an angry face. The cottage door opens onto impossible starlight inside. Forest twilight, fireflies.

stories.title: The Blue Cross
stories.author: G.K. Chesterton
stories.summary: A master thief disguised as a priest tries to steal a sapphire cross — but Father Brown is not as simple as he looks.
stories.description: The first Father Brown story. Aristide Valentin, the greatest detective in Europe, pursues Flambeau, the greatest thief, across London. But it is a small, shabby Catholic priest who catches him — not by being clever, but by understanding sin from the inside. Chesterton's detective solves crimes by understanding the human heart.
stories.authorBio: G.K. Chesterton (1874–1936) was an English writer, philosopher, and Catholic convert whose enormous body of work includes novels, essays, poems, and the beloved Father Brown detective stories. He wrote with wit, paradox, and a deep moral seriousness disguised as playfulness.
stories.relevance: Father Brown is a Catholic priest who solves crimes by understanding sin. These are detective stories with a moral compass — perfect for older children who love mysteries.
stories.coverScene: A small, round priest in a black cassock and shovel hat walking along a London street, carrying a brown paper parcel under one arm, looking utterly harmless and absent-minded. Behind him at a distance, a tall, elegant man in a top hat follows, watching intently. Gaslights, wet cobblestones, a London evening turning to dusk.

stories.title: The Hammer of God
stories.author: G.K. Chesterton
stories.summary: A man is killed by a hammer blow of impossible force, and Father Brown looks up at the church tower.
stories.description: A tale about the vertigo of pride — what happens when a man looks down on others from a great height and begins to think he is God. Father Brown solves it by recognizing the sin, not the clue.
stories.authorBio: G.K. Chesterton (1874–1936) was an English writer, philosopher, and Catholic convert whose enormous body of work includes novels, essays, poems, and the beloved Father Brown detective stories. He wrote with wit, paradox, and a deep moral seriousness disguised as playfulness.
stories.relevance: Father Brown is a Catholic priest who solves crimes by understanding sin. These are detective stories with a moral compass — perfect for older children who love mysteries.
stories.coverScene: A small, round priest in a black cassock standing in the shadow of a tall church steeple, looking up at the tower with a thoughtful frown, one hand on his umbrella. The church is a medieval English village church surrounded by gravestones. A single hammer lies on the grass near the church door. Overcast midday, grey stone against grey sky.

stories.title: The Invisible Man
stories.author: G.K. Chesterton
stories.summary: A man is murdered in a house watched from every angle — yet no one saw anyone enter. Father Brown explains that there is a man everyone sees and no one notices.
stories.description: One of the great locked-room mysteries, solved by Father Brown's understanding of who we overlook. The answer is simultaneously a detective trick and a moral lesson about the people we treat as invisible.
stories.authorBio: G.K. Chesterton (1874–1936) was an English writer, philosopher, and Catholic convert whose enormous body of work includes novels, essays, poems, and the beloved Father Brown detective stories. He wrote with wit, paradox, and a deep moral seriousness disguised as playfulness.
stories.relevance: Father Brown is a Catholic priest who solves crimes by understanding sin. These are detective stories with a moral compass — perfect for older children who love mysteries.
stories.coverScene: A small, round priest in a black cassock sitting in an armchair in a cozy study, looking at four other men who sit around him with puzzled expressions. On the table between them, a pile of letters and a stopped clock. Snow falls outside the window. The priest's face is mild but his eyes are sharp. Warm lamplight, cold night outside.

stories.title: Greybeards at Play
stories.author: G.K. Chesterton
stories.summary: Nonsense poems by Chesterton — about professors, old men, and the joy of being ridiculous.
stories.description: Chesterton's lightest work — four long nonsense poems written in the spirit of Edward Lear and Lewis Carroll. They are about the pleasure of absurdity and the wisdom of not taking yourself seriously. Chesterton was 26 when he wrote them and already knew that the most serious things are best said while laughing.
stories.authorBio: G.K. Chesterton (1874–1936) was an English writer, philosopher, and Catholic convert whose enormous body of work includes novels, essays, poems, and the beloved Father Brown detective stories. He wrote with wit, paradox, and a deep moral seriousness disguised as playfulness.
stories.relevance: Short, silly, and rhythmic — perfect for reading aloud when the evening needs to be lighter.
stories.coverScene: Three elderly gentlemen in Victorian morning coats and top hats playing leapfrog in a London park, their coattails flying, while a scandalized nanny pushes a pram past them. Pigeons scatter. A formal fountain splashes behind them. Bright, absurd, joyful afternoon light in a very proper setting.

stories.title: St. Francis of Assisi
stories.author: G.K. Chesterton
stories.summary: The story of Francis of Assisi begins — not with the saint, but with the troubadour, the soldier, and the rich man's son who gave it all away.
stories.description: Chesterton's biography of St. Francis is not a hagiography — it is a love letter from one extravagant personality to another. He writes about Francis as someone who saw the world as a gift that demanded gratitude, and who lived that gratitude with a recklessness that looked like madness. For older children, this is one of the great introductions to the saints.
stories.authorBio: G.K. Chesterton (1874–1936) was an English writer, philosopher, and Catholic convert whose enormous body of work includes novels, essays, poems, and the beloved Father Brown detective stories. He wrote with wit, paradox, and a deep moral seriousness disguised as playfulness.
stories.relevance: Chesterton makes Francis vivid and strange and real — not a plaster saint but a man on fire. For children old enough to wonder what holiness actually looks like.
stories.coverScene: A young man in a rich merchant's clothing standing in the town square of a medieval Italian hill town, arms thrown wide, looking up at the sky with an expression of sudden, overwhelming joy. Townspeople in the market stalls stare at him. Behind the town, the Umbrian hills glow green and gold. Brilliant Italian noon light.

stories.title: St. Francis of Assisi
stories.author: G.K. Chesterton
stories.summary: Francis strips himself naked in the town square, gives his clothes to his father, and walks into the woods to begin again.
stories.description: Chesterton's biography of St. Francis is not a hagiography — it is a love letter from one extravagant personality to another. He writes about Francis as someone who saw the world as a gift that demanded gratitude, and who lived that gratitude with a recklessness that looked like madness. For older children, this is one of the great introductions to the saints.
stories.authorBio: G.K. Chesterton (1874–1936) was an English writer, philosopher, and Catholic convert whose enormous body of work includes novels, essays, poems, and the beloved Father Brown detective stories. He wrote with wit, paradox, and a deep moral seriousness disguised as playfulness.
stories.relevance: Chesterton makes Francis vivid and strange and real — not a plaster saint but a man on fire. For children old enough to wonder what holiness actually looks like.
stories.coverScene: A thin young man in a rough brown robe tied with a rope belt, barefoot, kneeling in a meadow with birds perched on his outstretched arms and shoulders — a sparrow, a dove, a finch. A wolf sits peacefully beside him like a tame dog. Rolling Umbrian hills in the background, wildflowers everywhere. Soft golden morning light.

stories.title: The Snow Queen
stories.author: Hans Christian Andersen
stories.summary: A magical mirror shatters and a splinter lodges in the heart of a boy named Kai, turning him cold and cruel. His friend Gerda sets out on a long, brave journey to find him and bring him home.
stories.description: Andersen's longest and most ambitious fairy tale, told in seven stories. The Snow Queen is a tale about the power of love to thaw a frozen heart — Gerda's journey takes her through enchanted gardens, robber caves, and the frozen palace of the Snow Queen herself. It is the story that inspired Frozen, but the original is deeper, stranger, and more beautiful.
stories.authorBio: Hans Christian Andersen (1805–1875) was a Danish author whose fairy tales — The Ugly Duckling, The Little Mermaid, The Snow Queen, The Emperor's New Clothes — are among the most widely read stories in the world. His tales carry deep moral and often Christian themes of sacrifice, transformation, and redemption.
stories.relevance: A great quest story driven by love, not violence. Gerda's weapons are her innocence, her prayers, and her refusal to give up on the person she loves. The Christian imagery — Gerda reciting the Lord's Prayer to calm the snow-sprites — runs through the entire tale.
stories.coverScene: A boy and a girl sitting on a rooftop garden between two chimneys, tending a window box of roses, their faces close together and happy. Above them, unseen, a vast sleigh made of ice pulled by white reindeer glides through the winter sky, driven by a tall pale woman in a gown of frost and diamonds. Danish rooftops below, snowflakes beginning to fall.

stories.title: The Emperor's New Clothes
stories.author: Hans Christian Andersen
stories.summary: Two swindlers convince an emperor that they've made him invisible clothes — and only a child tells the truth.
stories.description: One of Andersen's most beloved fairy tales. His stories carry deep moral and often Christian themes — sacrifice, transformation, and the knowledge that true beauty is never on the surface.
stories.authorBio: Hans Christian Andersen (1805–1875) was a Danish author whose fairy tales — The Ugly Duckling, The Little Mermaid, The Snow Queen, The Emperor's New Clothes — are among the most widely read stories in the world. His tales carry deep moral and often Christian themes of sacrifice, transformation, and redemption.
stories.relevance: Andersen's fairy tales are bedtime classics — stories that children hear as adventures and adults hear as parables.
stories.coverScene: A pompous emperor in his underwear striding down a grand palace corridor, chin held high, while two sly tailors behind him mime holding up a nonexistent train. Courtiers line the walls, nodding and clapping enthusiastically. Through a window, a small boy in the crowd outside points and laughs. Marble floors, gold leaf, absurd grandeur.

stories.title: The Princess and the Pea
stories.author: Hans Christian Andersen
stories.summary: A prince searches the world for a real princess — and finds her by placing a pea under twenty mattresses.
stories.description: One of Andersen's most beloved fairy tales. His stories carry deep moral and often Christian themes — sacrifice, transformation, and the knowledge that true beauty is never on the surface.
stories.authorBio: Hans Christian Andersen (1805–1875) was a Danish author whose fairy tales — The Ugly Duckling, The Little Mermaid, The Snow Queen, The Emperor's New Clothes — are among the most widely read stories in the world. His tales carry deep moral and often Christian themes of sacrifice, transformation, and redemption.
stories.relevance: Andersen's fairy tales are bedtime classics — stories that children hear as adventures and adults hear as parables.
stories.coverScene: A girl standing at a castle door in a rainstorm, completely drenched — water dripping from her hair and clothes into puddles on the stone step — while a queen peers out at her with a suspicious expression. Behind the queen, servants carry an enormous stack of mattresses up a staircase. Lightning flashes. Heavy rain.

stories.title: The Ugly Duckling
stories.author: Hans Christian Andersen
stories.summary: A bird who doesn't fit in anywhere discovers he was never a duckling at all — he was a swan.
stories.description: One of Andersen's most beloved fairy tales. His stories carry deep moral and often Christian themes — sacrifice, transformation, and the knowledge that true beauty is never on the surface.
stories.authorBio: Hans Christian Andersen (1805–1875) was a Danish author whose fairy tales — The Ugly Duckling, The Little Mermaid, The Snow Queen, The Emperor's New Clothes — are among the most widely read stories in the world. His tales carry deep moral and often Christian themes of sacrifice, transformation, and redemption.
stories.relevance: Andersen's fairy tales are bedtime classics — stories that children hear as adventures and adults hear as parables.
stories.coverScene: A small, scruffy grey bird sitting alone at the edge of a farm pond, head tucked low, while a family of fluffy yellow ducklings paddles away from it. A mother duck herds the yellow ducklings toward the reeds. The grey bird's reflection in the water is sad. Overcast day, autumn reeds turning brown.

stories.title: Thumbelina
stories.author: Hans Christian Andersen
stories.summary: A tiny girl no bigger than a thumb is carried away by a toad, a beetle, and a mole before finding her true home.
stories.description: One of Andersen's most beloved fairy tales. His stories carry deep moral and often Christian themes — sacrifice, transformation, and the knowledge that true beauty is never on the surface.
stories.authorBio: Hans Christian Andersen (1805–1875) was a Danish author whose fairy tales — The Ugly Duckling, The Little Mermaid, The Snow Queen, The Emperor's New Clothes — are among the most widely read stories in the world. His tales carry deep moral and often Christian themes of sacrifice, transformation, and redemption.
stories.relevance: Andersen's fairy tales are bedtime classics — stories that children hear as adventures and adults hear as parables.
stories.coverScene: A tiny girl the size of a thumb sitting inside an open tulip, her legs dangling over the petal edge, looking out at an enormous garden that stretches like a countryside around her. A bumblebee the size of her head hovers nearby. Dewdrops on the petals catch the morning light. Everything huge, everything magical.

stories.title: The Children's Bible
stories.author: Henry A. Sherman
stories.summary: In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth — light and darkness, sea and land, birds and beasts, and finally man and woman in His own image.
stories.description: Sherman and Kent's Children's Bible retells the greatest stories of the Old and New Testaments in clear, dignified language accessible to young readers. Each chapter stands alone as a complete story, making it ideal for bedtime reading.
stories.authorBio: Henry A. Sherman and Charles Foster Kent compiled The Children's Bible in 1922, selecting and retelling the greatest stories of the Old and New Testaments in language accessible to young readers. The result is a readable, dignified introduction to Scripture.
stories.relevance: Scripture retold for children — the foundational stories of Western civilization presented with clarity and reverence. Perfect for families who want to share Bible stories at bedtime.
stories.coverScene: A bearded man in a long robe standing on a hilltop with arms spread wide, looking out over a newly created world — green valleys, a winding river, animals of every kind moving through meadows below. Light breaks through parting clouds in golden rays. The man's expression is one of wonder. Dawn of the first morning.

stories.title: The Children's Bible
stories.author: Henry A. Sherman
stories.summary: The story of the Garden of Eden — Adam and Eve, the serpent's temptation, the forbidden fruit, and the first exile from paradise.
stories.description: Sherman and Kent's Children's Bible retells the greatest stories of the Old and New Testaments in clear, dignified language accessible to young readers. Each chapter stands alone as a complete story, making it ideal for bedtime reading.
stories.authorBio: Henry A. Sherman and Charles Foster Kent compiled The Children's Bible in 1922, selecting and retelling the greatest stories of the Old and New Testaments in language accessible to young readers. The result is a readable, dignified introduction to Scripture.
stories.relevance: Scripture retold for children — the foundational stories of Western civilization presented with clarity and reverence. Perfect for families who want to share Bible stories at bedtime.
stories.coverScene: A boy in a simple tunic kneeling beside a stream, filling a leather sling with a smooth stone, while across the stream a towering armored giant — seen only from the waist down, enormous greaves and sandals — casts a shadow over the boy. The boy looks up without fear. Rocky valley, bright harsh sunlight.

stories.title: The Story-Book of Science
stories.author: Jean-Henri Fabre
stories.summary: Uncle Paul continues his lessons about the natural world — explaining the wonders of insects, plants, or minerals with the patience and enthusiasm of the born teacher.
