stories.description: The second chapter of Fabre's nature classic. Uncle Paul answers his niece's and nephew's questions about the world by telling them stories — stories that are also precise, beautiful natural history. Fabre believed that wonder was the beginning of all knowledge.
stories.authorBio: Jean-Henri Fabre (1823–1915) was a French naturalist and author whose patient observation of insects earned him the nickname 'the Homer of the insects.' The Story-Book of Science presents his discoveries as conversations between an uncle and his niece and nephew — gentle, precise, and full of wonder.
stories.relevance: Science told as bedtime stories — gentle, precise, and full of wonder. Fabre's approach to nature is exactly right for children: patient observation, vivid description, and a sense that the smallest insect is a marvel.
stories.coverScene: Two children lying on their stomachs in tall grass, chins on hands, watching a spider spin a web between two stems of wild fennel. An old man in a straw hat crouches behind them, pointing out the spider's technique. Morning dew on the web catches the light like tiny diamonds. Southern French countryside.

stories.title: The Story Book of Science
stories.author: Jean-Henri Fabre
stories.summary: Uncle Paul tells his nieces and nephews about the caterpillar — and the miracle of what it becomes.
stories.description: From The Story Book of Science — stories about the natural world told with reverence and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Jean-Henri Fabre (1823–1915) was a French naturalist who spent his life observing insects with a reverence for creation that made him beloved by Catholic homeschoolers. His Story Book of Science presents science as conversations between a wise uncle and his nieces and nephews.
stories.relevance: Nature stories for bedtime — because the world outside the window is as full of stories as any fairy tale.
stories.coverScene: An old man in a wide-brimmed straw hat sitting on a garden bench with two children, pointing with a magnifying glass at a caterpillar inching along a leaf held in the boy's hand. The girl leans in close, fascinated. A Provençal garden with lavender, stone walls, and cypress trees. Warm French afternoon light.

stories.title: Peter and Wendy
stories.author: J.M. Barrie
stories.summary: A boy who never grows up flies to the nursery window and takes three children to a land where no one has to be old.
stories.description: The first chapter of Peter and Wendy — the novel, not the play. Barrie's prose is stranger and sadder and funnier than any adaptation. This is the real Peter Pan, told in a voice that winks at parents over children's heads.
stories.authorBio: J.M. Barrie (1860–1937) was a Scottish novelist and playwright who created Peter Pan, first as a play in 1904, then as a novel in 1911. The story grew from tales he told to the Llewelyn Davies boys, whom he eventually adopted.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.
stories.coverScene: A boy in ragged green leaves crouching on a windowsill of a London nursery at night, silhouetted against a huge full moon, while three children sleep in white iron beds inside. A tiny light — a fairy — hovers by his shoulder. The nursery has a rocking horse, a dollhouse, and wallpaper with sailing ships. Cold moonlight outside, warm nightlight within.

stories.title: Heidi
stories.author: Johanna Spyri
stories.summary: Five-year-old Heidi is brought by her aunt to live with her grandfather — the gruff, solitary Alm-Uncle — high in the Swiss Alps. The mountain air, the goats, and the old man's hidden kindness begin to work their magic.
stories.description: The opening chapter of one of the most beloved children's novels ever written. Heidi arrives in the Alps — a place of extraordinary natural beauty described with Spyri's characteristic warmth. The grandfather, who has withdrawn from the world, begins to thaw in the presence of this irrepressible child.
stories.authorBio: Johanna Spyri (1827–1901) was a Swiss author best known for Heidi, her beloved novel about a young orphan girl who goes to live with her grandfather in the Swiss Alps. A devout Christian, Spyri filled the book with themes of faith, nature, healing, and the goodness of simple living.
stories.relevance: A story about the healing power of nature, faith, and love. Heidi's joy is infectious — she loves the Alps with her whole heart, and her love transforms everyone around her. A profoundly Christian story told through the lens of a child's wonder.
stories.coverScene: A small girl with curly hair and bare feet climbing a steep Alpine meadow path, leading an old woman by the hand. Above them, a small wooden chalet with a single chimney sits on a high pasture. White goats graze on the slope. The Alps rise snow-capped behind everything. Brilliant blue sky, wildflowers in the grass, clean mountain light.

stories.title: The Pilgrim's Progress (for Young Readers)
stories.author: John Bunyan
stories.summary: Christian sets out from the City of Destruction to find the Celestial City — told in words of one syllable.
stories.description: Lucy Aikin's ingenious adaptation of Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress, retold entirely in words of one syllable. The great Christian allegory — the Slough of Despond, Vanity Fair, the Delectable Mountains — made accessible to the youngest readers. The simplicity of the language gives it an unexpected power.
stories.authorBio: John Bunyan (1628–1688) was an English preacher and writer who wrote The Pilgrim's Progress while imprisoned for his religious convictions. Lucy Aikin adapted it for young children, preserving the story while simplifying the language.
stories.relevance: The greatest Christian allegory, told simply enough for a five-year-old. A journey story for bedtime — one chapter per night, one step closer to the Celestial City.
stories.coverScene: A man in rough traveling clothes carrying an enormous pack on his back, standing at a fork in a dirt road, reading a worn book held in both hands. One road leads into a dark valley; the other climbs toward a distant hilltop city shining with golden light. The man's face shows fear and determination in equal measure. Dramatic sky, light breaking through clouds.

stories.title: Little Pictorial Lives of the Saints
stories.author: John Gilmary Shea
stories.summary: A short, vivid biography of a saint — their life, their virtues, and how they served God. Each entry was written to be read aloud on the saint's feast day.
stories.description: From Little Pictorial Lives of the Saints. Shea's collection covers hundreds of saints across the liturgical calendar. Each biography is brief — perfect for a bedtime reading — and written to inspire imitation of the saint's particular virtue: courage, patience, charity, or faith.
stories.authorBio: John Gilmary Shea (1824–1892) was an American Catholic historian who compiled Little Pictorial Lives of the Saints, a collection of brief biographies of the saints organized by their feast days. The entries are short, vivid, and written to inspire — perfect for reading aloud at bedtime.
stories.relevance: A short daily reading that connects children to the liturgical calendar and the communion of saints. Each saint's story is a real-life adventure in holiness.
stories.coverScene: A row of small illustrated panels on a golden background, each showing a different saint — a woman holding a palm branch, a bishop in a mitre, a monk reading a book, a young girl with a lantern. The panels are framed like illuminated manuscript miniatures. Rich gold, deep blue, and crimson. Medieval devotional warmth.

stories.title: St. George, Martyr
stories.author: John Gilmary Shea
stories.summary: The life of St. George — the soldier-saint whose courage in the face of persecution made him the patron of England, and whose legend gave us the story of the dragon.
stories.description: From Little Pictorial Lives of the Saints. The historical St. George was a Roman soldier who refused to renounce his Christian faith under Diocletian's persecution. His legend — the knight who slew the dragon to save the princess — became one of the most enduring images of Christian courage in Western art and literature.
stories.authorBio: John Gilmary Shea (1824–1892) was an American Catholic historian who compiled Little Pictorial Lives of the Saints, a collection of brief biographies of the saints organized by their feast days. The entries are short, vivid, and written to inspire — perfect for reading aloud at bedtime.
stories.relevance: The patron saint of England, scouts, and soldiers. For children, St. George is the original dragon-slayer — a hero who fights evil not for glory but for love and faith.
stories.coverScene: A knight in silver armor on a white horse, lance lowered, charging toward a green dragon that rears up with wings spread and fire curling from its jaws. A princess in a white dress watches from behind a rock. Rolling green English countryside behind them. Dramatic light, the knight's red cross banner flying.

stories.title: St. Patrick, Apostle of Ireland
stories.author: John Gilmary Shea
stories.summary: The life of St. Patrick — born in Roman Britain, enslaved in Ireland as a boy, and called back by God to convert the Irish nation to Christianity.
stories.description: From Little Pictorial Lives of the Saints. Patrick's story is one of the great adventure-and-faith narratives of the Christian tradition: captured by pirates at sixteen, enslaved for six years, escaped, and then — astonishingly — returned to the land of his captivity to bring the Gospel.
stories.authorBio: John Gilmary Shea (1824–1892) was an American Catholic historian who compiled Little Pictorial Lives of the Saints, a collection of brief biographies of the saints organized by their feast days. The entries are short, vivid, and written to inspire — perfect for reading aloud at bedtime.
stories.relevance: One of the most beloved saints in the world, especially for children with Irish heritage. His feast day (March 17) is celebrated globally. A story of courage, forgiveness, and radical obedience to God's call.
stories.coverScene: A man in bishop's robes standing on a green Irish hillside, holding a shamrock in one hand and a wooden staff in the other, teaching a small crowd of people in rough Celtic cloaks. Stone crosses and round towers dot the landscape behind him. Sheep on the hillside. Soft grey-green Irish light, rain just passed.

stories.title: The King of the Golden River
stories.author: John Ruskin
stories.summary: The two cruel older brothers each attempt the journey to the Golden River, but their selfishness undoes them. Only little Gluck, the youngest and kindest, has a chance.
stories.description: The second chapter of Ruskin's fairy tale. Three brothers must climb to the source of the Golden River. The cruel brothers fail because they refuse to share their water with those in need along the way. Gluck, the kind youngest brother, gives away everything — and is rewarded.
stories.authorBio: John Ruskin (1819–1900) was an English art critic, social thinker, and author. The King of the Golden River, written for a young friend, is his only fairy tale — a story about three brothers, kindness, greed, and a magical river of gold in the Austrian Alps.
stories.relevance: A fairy tale with a crystal-clear moral: kindness and generosity are rewarded; selfishness and cruelty are punished. The structure — three brothers, three attempts — is classic fairy-tale architecture that children find deeply satisfying.

series.name: The King of the Golden River

stories.coverScene: A small boy climbing a steep, rocky mountain path beside a rushing stream, carrying a golden flask. Above him, the stream becomes a waterfall that pours down from peaks lost in clouds. A tiny golden figure sits cross-legged on a boulder mid-stream, watching the boy approach with ancient, knowing eyes. Late afternoon mountain light, mist from the falls.

stories.title: The King of the Golden River
stories.author: John Ruskin
stories.summary: Two cruel brothers and one kind one seek the Golden River — and only kindness finds the way.
stories.description: Ruskin's only fairy tale, written in 1841 for a twelve-year-old girl. Three brothers live in a beautiful valley. Two are cruel; one is kind. When the valley is cursed, each sets out to find the King of the Golden River. The cruel brothers fail in ways that are darkly funny; the kind brother succeeds by giving away his water to those who need it more. A perfect moral tale.
stories.authorBio: John Ruskin (1819–1900) was an English art critic, social thinker, and writer who championed beauty, craftsmanship, and moral seriousness. He wrote The King of the Golden River at 22 — his only fairy tale, and one of the finest in English.
stories.relevance: A fairy tale about generosity — short enough for one evening, deep enough to remember.

series.name: The King of the Golden River

stories.coverScene: Two brutish-looking men sitting at a kitchen table in a mountain cottage, eating and drinking greedily, while a small, kind-faced boy sweeps the hearth behind them. Through the window, a magnificent Alpine valley stretches green and golden under a waterfall that catches the sunset light. The contrast between the brothers' gloom and the valley's beauty is stark.

stories.title: Celtic Fairy Tales
stories.author: Joseph Jacobs
stories.summary: A tale from the Celtic twilight — where the fair folk live just beyond the edge of the world we can see.
stories.description: From Celtic Fairy Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: Joseph Jacobs collected Celtic fairy tales from Ireland, Scotland, and Wales — stories of enchantment, heroes, and the otherworld that have been told in the British Isles for a thousand years.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.
stories.coverScene: A young man in a rough Irish cloak standing on a sea cliff, looking down at a seal woman sitting on the rocks below, her sealskin draped over a boulder beside her. Waves crash against the cliff base. The woman looks up at him with dark, liquid eyes. Wild Atlantic coast, grey-green sea, wind-torn clouds.

stories.title: English Fairy Tales
stories.author: Joseph Jacobs
stories.summary: Jack sells the cow for magic beans — and climbs the beanstalk to find a giant's castle in the clouds.
stories.description: From English Fairy Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: Joseph Jacobs (1854–1916) was an Australian-born folklorist who collected and published the fairy tales of England, Ireland, and India with scholarly rigor and a storyteller's ear.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.
stories.coverScene: A boy in tattered clothes climbing an enormous beanstalk, one hand gripping a thick green stem, peering up through broad leaves at a castle floating among the clouds above. His cottage and a small cow are tiny specks far below. Bright sky, towering cumulus clouds, the beanstalk disappearing into mist.

stories.title: The Wind in the Willows
stories.author: Kenneth Grahame
stories.summary: Mole abandons his spring cleaning, meets the Water Rat, and discovers the joys of the river.
stories.description: Chapter 1 — 'The River Bank.' On the first warm day of spring, Mole throws down his whitewash brush, runs out of his underground home and finds himself for the first time at the edge of a river. He meets Ratty, who introduces him to messing about in boats, packs a fat picnic basket, and the two of them drift through a slow golden afternoon while Ratty tells him about all the strange and brave creatures of the riverbank — including, in passing, a certain Mr Toad. It is one of the gentlest and most contented openings in English children's literature.
stories.authorBio: Kenneth Grahame (1859–1932) was a Scottish writer who spent his working life as Secretary of the Bank of England. He wrote The Wind in the Willows for his young son Alastair, originally as bedtime stories and then as letters when Alastair was away. It was published in 1908 and rejected by several editors before becoming one of the most beloved books in the English language.
stories.relevance: A book about friendship, the seasons, and the deep pleasure of small things — a riverbank, a picnic, a quiet afternoon. Perfect for the slow chapter-a-night pace bedtime asks for.

series.name: The Wind in the Willows
series.nextTeaser: Mole and Ratty pay a visit to the boastful Mr Toad, who has discovered an entirely new and very expensive enthusiasm: the gypsy caravan.

stories.coverScene: A small brown mole in a blue smock standing at the entrance of his burrow, blinking in the spring sunlight, a whitewash brush still in his paw. A river sparkles beyond the meadow. A water rat in a striped blazer waves to him from a rowboat tied at the bank. Wildflowers, willows, first warm day of spring.

stories.title: The Wind in the Willows
stories.author: Kenneth Grahame
stories.summary: Toad introduces Ratty and Mole to his latest obsession — a canary-yellow caravan and the open road. But the first motor-car they meet changes everything.
stories.description: Kenneth Grahame's immortal tale of four friends on the riverbank — Mole, Ratty, Badger, and the irrepressible Toad. The book is about friendship, home, adventure, and the English countryside, written with a richness of language that rewards being read aloud.
stories.authorBio: Kenneth Grahame (1859–1932) was a Scottish writer who spent his working life as Secretary of the Bank of England. He wrote The Wind in the Willows for his young son Alastair, originally as bedtime stories and then as letters. It was published in 1908 and became one of the most beloved books in the English language.
stories.relevance: One of the great bedtime books — each chapter is a self-contained adventure in the life of the riverbank. The language is gorgeous, the characters are unforgettable, and the pace is perfect for reading aloud at the end of the day.
stories.coverScene: A mole in a blue smock and a water rat in a striped blazer walking along a country road at twilight, approaching the open gate of a grand estate. Beyond the gate, a handsome red motorcar is parked on the gravel drive. A toad in a tweed jacket and driving goggles leans against the car, beaming. Warm evening light, English country house.

stories.title: The Wind in the Willows
stories.author: Kenneth Grahame
stories.summary: Mole ventures alone into the Wild Wood in winter and discovers why sensible animals stay home. The snow, the eyes in the dark, and at last — Badger's door.
stories.description: Kenneth Grahame's immortal tale of four friends on the riverbank — Mole, Ratty, Badger, and the irrepressible Toad. The book is about friendship, home, adventure, and the English countryside, written with a richness of language that rewards being read aloud.
stories.authorBio: Kenneth Grahame (1859–1932) was a Scottish writer who spent his working life as Secretary of the Bank of England. He wrote The Wind in the Willows for his young son Alastair, originally as bedtime stories and then as letters. It was published in 1908 and became one of the most beloved books in the English language.
stories.relevance: One of the great bedtime books — each chapter is a self-contained adventure in the life of the riverbank. The language is gorgeous, the characters are unforgettable, and the pace is perfect for reading aloud at the end of the day.
stories.coverScene: A mole in a blue smock lost in a dark, tangled forest at night, tree roots catching at his feet, eyes glinting from hollow trees around him. In the distance, a faint warm light glows through the trees — the badger's door. Snow beginning to fall. The mole looks small and frightened but presses on toward the light.

stories.title: Rikki-Tikki-Tavi
stories.author: Rudyard Kipling
stories.summary: A brave young mongoose protects a family from two deadly cobras in their garden.
stories.description: Rikki-tikki-tavi is a young mongoose washed out of his burrow by a flood and adopted by a British family in India. When two cobras — the cunning Nag and his mate Nagaina — threaten the family, Rikki-tikki fights them with courage far beyond his size. Kipling packs more suspense into this single chapter of The Jungle Book than most novels manage in three hundred pages.
stories.authorBio: Rudyard Kipling (1865–1936) was a British author and poet born in Bombay during the Raj. He wrote The Jungle Book, Kim, and Just So Stories, and became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature.
stories.relevance: One of the greatest adventure stories ever written for children. Rikki-tikki is small but fearless — exactly the kind of hero children root for at bedtime.
stories.coverScene: A small brown mongoose with bristling fur and bright red eyes facing down a large black cobra in a moonlit garden, both animals tense and coiled. The cobra's hood is spread wide. Behind the mongoose, a bungalow with a lit veranda where a small boy watches through the screen door. Indian garden, jasmine vines, warm tropical night.

stories.title: A Mad Tea-Party
stories.author: Lewis Carroll
stories.summary: Alice sits down to tea with a Hatter, a March Hare, and a Dormouse — and nothing makes any sense at all.
stories.description: From Alice's Adventures in Wonderland by Lewis Carroll.
stories.authorBio: Lewis Carroll was the pen name of Charles Lutwidge Dodgson (1832–1898), an English mathematician, photographer, and Anglican deacon at Christ Church, Oxford. He invented the Wonderland stories on a boat trip up the Thames for the daughters of his friend — Alice Liddell among them.
stories.relevance: Part of the beloved Alice's Adventures in Wonderland — perfect for chapter-a-night bedtime reading.
stories.coverScene: A girl in a blue dress sitting at a long outdoor table set for tea, squeezed between a man in an enormous top hat and a March hare, while a dormouse sleeps in the teapot between them. Stacked teacups, spilled sugar, bread and butter everywhere. The Hatter gestures wildly. A garden of painted roses behind them. Afternoon light, cheerful chaos.

stories.title: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
stories.author: Lewis Carroll
stories.summary: Alice follows a waistcoated rabbit down a hole and falls, and falls, and falls.
stories.description: Chapter 1 — 'Down the Rabbit-Hole.' Alice is sitting on a riverbank with her sister, getting bored, when a White Rabbit in a waistcoat hurries past muttering 'Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be late!' She follows him into a hole and finds herself falling down a long, slow tunnel, with time enough to pick a jar of orange marmalade off a passing shelf, wonder how many miles she has fallen, and worry about her cat Dinah. When she lands she finds a tiny door, a bottle labelled 'DRINK ME,' and the start of every strange and reasonable thing that follows.
stories.authorBio: Lewis Carroll was the pen name of Charles Lutwidge Dodgson (1832–1898), an English mathematician, photographer, and Anglican deacon at Christ Church, Oxford. He invented the Wonderland stories on a boat trip up the Thames for the daughters of his friend, the dean — Alice Liddell among them. He wrote them down because Alice asked him to.
stories.relevance: Wonderland is one of those books that lives in the rhythm of the sentences. It rewards being read aloud slowly, with patience for the jokes that work on grown-ups too.

series.name: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
series.nextTeaser: Alice has cried herself a great pool of tears, met a startled mouse, and finds herself at the start of 'a Caucus-race and a Long Tale.'

stories.coverScene: A girl in a blue dress and white pinafore falling slowly down a very deep well shaft, her skirt billowing around her, passing shelves lined with marmalade jars, maps, and bookshelves. A white rabbit in a waistcoat clutching a pocket watch tumbles ahead of her. The shaft narrows into darkness below. Dreamlike, slow-motion light.

stories.title: The Pool of Tears
stories.author: Lewis Carroll
stories.summary: Alice has cried herself an ocean and meets a mouse swimming in it.
stories.description: From Alice's Adventures in Wonderland by Lewis Carroll.
stories.authorBio: Lewis Carroll was the pen name of Charles Lutwidge Dodgson (1832–1898), an English mathematician, photographer, and Anglican deacon at Christ Church, Oxford. He invented the Wonderland stories on a boat trip up the Thames for the daughters of his friend — Alice Liddell among them.
stories.relevance: Part of the beloved Alice's Adventures in Wonderland — perfect for chapter-a-night bedtime reading.
stories.coverScene: A very tall girl — her head brushing the ceiling — sitting on the floor of a tiny hallway, crying enormous tears that pool around her. A white rabbit in a waistcoat scurries past through the tears. A tiny golden key lies on a glass table high above. The tears are forming an actual pool. Absurd, melancholy, whimsical.

stories.title: Anne of Green Gables
stories.author: L.M. Montgomery
stories.summary: Mrs. Rachel Lynde sees Matthew Cuthbert driving to the station — and cannot imagine what business he could have there.
stories.description: The first chapter of Anne of Green Gables. Matthew and Marilla Cuthbert have asked for an orphan boy to help on the farm. What arrives is a red-haired girl who talks without stopping and names everything she sees. Montgomery's prose is as full of flowers as Anne's imagination.
stories.authorBio: L.M. Montgomery (1874–1942) was a Canadian author who grew up on Prince Edward Island and set most of her novels there. Anne of Green Gables was published in 1908 and has sold over 50 million copies.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.
stories.coverScene: A thin, red-haired girl with freckles sitting on a worn suitcase at a rural train station platform, looking eagerly down the road. She wears a faded dress and a straw hat. Behind her, a Prince Edward Island landscape of red dirt roads, green fields, and white-blossomed apple orchards stretches to the sea. Late spring afternoon, warm light on the red hair.

stories.title: Anne of Green Gables
stories.author: L.M. Montgomery
stories.summary: Matthew Cuthbert arrives at the station expecting a boy and finds instead a thin, red-haired girl with an enormous imagination and an unstoppable flow of words.
stories.description: The second chapter of Anne of Green Gables. Anne Shirley's first appearance is one of the great character introductions in children's literature — by the time Matthew drives her home, the reader is as won over as he is.
stories.authorBio: Lucy Maud Montgomery (1874–1942) was a Canadian author best known for Anne of Green Gables, published in 1908. Anne Shirley — red-haired, imaginative, irrepressible — became one of the most beloved characters in children's literature.
stories.relevance: Anne's irrepressible imagination and hunger for beauty make her one of the most beloved characters in children's literature. Her story is about finding where you belong.
stories.coverScene: A thin, red-haired girl sitting in the back of a horse-drawn buggy beside a stern, quiet man in a straw hat, her face alight with wonder as they drive along a road canopied by blooming apple trees — the petals drifting down like snow. Red dirt road, white farmhouses, the sparkle of the sea in the distance. Prince Edward Island in full spring bloom.

stories.title: The Wonder Book of Bible Stories
stories.author: Logan Marshall
stories.summary: The great stories of the Old and New Testaments retold as vivid narratives for young readers.
stories.description: Logan Marshall's 1917 retelling of the Bible's greatest stories — Abraham and Isaac, Joseph and his brothers, Moses and the Red Sea, David and Goliath, Daniel in the lion's den, the birth of Christ, and many more. Written as real stories with character and drama, not as Sunday school summaries.
stories.authorBio: Logan Marshall compiled these Bible stories for young readers in the early twentieth century.
stories.relevance: Scripture told as story — the way it was meant to be heard at bedtime.

series.name: The Wonder Book of Bible Stories

stories.coverScene: A boy in a simple tunic standing in a field of golden wheat, arms raised toward the sky, where the sun, moon, and eleven stars appear to bow down toward him. His older brothers — rough, bearded men — watch from the edge of the field with jealous, angry expressions. Wide biblical landscape, dramatic sky.

stories.title: The Wonder Book of Bible Stories
stories.author: Logan Marshall
stories.summary: The great stories of the Old and New Testaments retold as vivid narratives for young readers.
stories.description: Logan Marshall's 1917 retelling of the Bible's greatest stories — Abraham and Isaac, Joseph and his brothers, Moses and the Red Sea, David and Goliath, Daniel in the lion's den, the birth of Christ, and many more. Written as real stories with character and drama, not as Sunday school summaries.
stories.authorBio: Logan Marshall compiled these Bible stories for young readers in the early twentieth century.
stories.relevance: Scripture told as story — the way it was meant to be heard at bedtime.

series.name: The Wonder Book of Bible Stories

stories.coverScene: A baby in a woven reed basket floating among tall papyrus reeds at the edge of a great river, the basket gently rocking on the water. A young woman in Egyptian linen — a princess — wades toward the basket, her handmaidens watching from the bank. Warm Egyptian sunlight, the river golden and slow.

stories.title: Little Women
stories.author: Louisa May Alcott
stories.summary: Four sisters — Meg, Jo, Beth, and Amy March — sit by the fire on Christmas Eve, poor but together.
stories.description: The opening chapter of Little Women — 'Playing Pilgrims.' The March sisters are poor, their father is away at war, and Christmas looks bleak. But they have each other and their mother's copy of Pilgrim's Progress, and the chapter sets the tone for the whole book: virtue is not about having things but about how you carry their absence.
stories.authorBio: Louisa May Alcott (1832–1888) was an American novelist raised in a Transcendentalist household by her father Bronson Alcott. She wrote Little Women in 1868 based on her own family, and it became one of the most beloved American novels.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.
stories.coverScene: Four sisters sitting around a small fireplace in a modest parlor — the eldest knitting, the second reading with her feet up, the third sketching on a pad, and the youngest sprawled on the rug playing with a cat. A framed photograph of a man in a Union Army uniform hangs on the wall. Warm but spare room. Christmas Eve, lamplight, gentle snowfall visible through the window.

stories.title: Parables from Nature
stories.author: Margaret Gatty
stories.summary: A caterpillar who cannot imagine being a butterfly — a parable about faith and transformation.
stories.description: Margaret Gatty's Parables from Nature draws moral lessons from the natural world — a caterpillar who doubts the promise of wings, a flower that grows in the dark, water that finds its level. Written explicitly for children and beloved by Victorian families, these are nature stories with a quiet Christian spine.
stories.authorBio: Margaret Gatty (1809–1873) was an English writer, naturalist, and editor of Aunt Judy's Magazine. Her Parables from Nature was used in schools and homes throughout the Victorian era as moral instruction drawn from science.
stories.relevance: Stories that teach virtue through nature — for the child who asks 'why?' about the world outside.
stories.coverScene: A caterpillar and a butterfly having a conversation on a leaf at the edge of a pond — the caterpillar looking up skeptically at the butterfly, which has alighted on a reed above. Lily pads, dragonflies, water reflections. The butterfly's wings are iridescent blue. Soft morning light on the water.

stories.title: Parables from Nature
stories.author: Margaret Gatty
stories.summary: Another parable drawn from the natural world — seeds, insects, rivers, or crystals — teaching a moral lesson through the patient observation of creation.
stories.description: The second selection from Margaret Gatty's Parables from Nature. Gatty was a naturalist as well as a moralist, and her parables work because the natural phenomena she describes are real and genuinely fascinating. The moral grows organically from the observation.
stories.authorBio: Margaret Gatty (1809–1873) was an English author and naturalist. Her Parables from Nature used the natural world — seeds, insects, rivers, crystals — to teach moral and spiritual lessons. The parables are elegant and accessible, written for children but enjoyed by adults.
stories.relevance: Nature as moral teacher — Gatty's approach is gentle and intelligent. She doesn't force lessons onto nature; she shows how the natural world already embodies the virtues she wants to teach.
stories.coverScene: A small seedling pushing up through dark earth, its first two leaves unfolding toward a beam of sunlight that breaks through forest canopy above. Earthworms curl around roots below the soil line — the scene is split to show both above and below ground. Rich dark earth, pale green shoot, golden light from above.

stories.title: The Adventures of Tom Sawyer
stories.author: Mark Twain
stories.summary: Tom Sawyer plays hooky, gets in trouble with Aunt Polly, and begins the great fence-whitewashing episode — convincing the entire neighborhood that painting a fence is a privilege, not a punishment.
stories.description: The opening of Mark Twain's classic American novel. Tom Sawyer is the original lovable troublemaker — smart, lazy, imaginative, and operating by a code of honor that makes perfect sense to every boy who ever lived. The fence-whitewashing scene is one of the most famous episodes in American literature.
stories.authorBio: Mark Twain was the pen name of Samuel Clemens (1835–1910), an American writer, humorist, and lecturer widely regarded as the father of American literature. Tom Sawyer, published in 1876, draws on his boyhood in Hannibal, Missouri — a childhood spent exploring caves, rafting, and getting into exactly the kind of trouble Tom gets into.
stories.relevance: The great American boyhood novel. Tom's adventures on the Mississippi are funny, exciting, and deeply rooted in a specific time and place — but his spirit is universal. Every child recognizes Tom's mixture of cleverness and mischief.
stories.coverScene: A barefoot boy in overalls and a straw hat holding a paintbrush loaded with whitewash, grinning slyly at another boy who approaches along a wooden fence carrying an apple. The fence stretches along a dusty Missouri street. The first boy has already painted a small section white. Hot summer afternoon, cicada weather.

stories.title: The House at Pooh Corner: An Enchanted Place on the Top of the Forest
stories.author: A. A. Milne
stories.summary: Christopher Robin and Pooh visit their enchanted place one last time before Christopher Robin goes away.
stories.description: The final chapter of the Pooh stories, and one of the most moving passages in children's literature. Christopher Robin is going away — to school, though neither he nor Pooh quite says it — and they walk together to their enchanted place at the top of the forest. The tenderness of the goodbye lingers long after the story ends.
stories.authorBio: A. A. Milne (1882–1956) was an English playwright and novelist who created Winnie-the-Pooh for his son Christopher Robin.
stories.relevance: A beautiful, bittersweet ending that parents may find as moving as children find comforting. A perfect final-chapter bedtime listen.
stories.coverScene: A boy in shorts and a sun hat and a small round bear in a red shirt sitting side by side on the top of a grassy hill, looking out over the Hundred Acre Wood spread below them. The boy's arm rests on the bear. Neither speaks. The light is golden and autumnal, the trees turning. A bittersweet, beautiful stillness.

stories.title: The House at Pooh Corner: Tigger Comes to the Forest
stories.author: A. A. Milne
stories.summary: A new animal called Tigger bounces into the Hundred Acre Wood and tries to find what Tiggers like to eat.
stories.description: Tigger arrives in the Forest — bouncy, confident, and completely certain he likes everything. One by one he tries honey, acorns, thistles, and more, only to discover that what Tiggers really like is Roo's Extract of Malt. Milne introduces his most exuberant character in a chapter that's pure delight read aloud.
stories.authorBio: A. A. Milne (1882–1956) was an English playwright and novelist who created Winnie-the-Pooh for his son Christopher Robin.
stories.relevance: Tigger's boundless energy and the gentle way the Forest creatures help him is a warm, funny nighttime listen.
stories.coverScene: A bouncy orange-and-black striped tiger sitting at a table in a cozy tree-house kitchen, turning up his nose at a pot of honey while a tiny kangaroo offers him a spoonful of something from a jar. A small round bear in a red shirt watches from the doorway. Warm kitchen light, jars and pots on shelves.

stories.title: Winnie-the-Pooh: We Are Introduced to Pooh and Some Bees
stories.author: A. A. Milne
stories.summary: Christopher Robin's bear of very little brain tries to reach a bee's nest by floating on a balloon — and gets stuck.
stories.description: The opening chapter of one of the most beloved children's books ever written. Edward Bear, known to his friends as Winnie-the-Pooh, concocts a scheme involving a balloon and a mud disguise to reach honey in a tall tree. Milne's prose is warm, unhurried, and full of quiet jokes — the voice of a father telling a bedtime story to his son.
stories.authorBio: A. A. Milne (1882–1956) was an English playwright and novelist who created Winnie-the-Pooh for his son Christopher Robin. The stories, set in the Hundred Acre Wood, have become some of the most recognized children's literature in the world.
stories.relevance: Pooh is the quintessential bedtime companion — gentle, funny, and reassuringly predictable. These chapters work beautifully as standalone stories.

series.name: Winnie-the-Pooh
series.nextTeaser: Next: Pooh goes visiting Rabbit and eats too much honey.

stories.coverScene: A small, round bear in a red shirt dangling from a blue balloon high above a tall oak tree, one paw reaching toward a hole in the trunk where bees buzz in and out. A boy in shorts and a sun hat watches from the ground below, shielding his eyes. The Hundred Acre Wood stretches behind them — gentle green hills and silver birches. Warm summer afternoon.

stories.title: Winnie-the-Pooh: Pooh Goes Visiting and Gets Into a Tight Place
stories.author: A. A. Milne
stories.summary: Pooh visits Rabbit's home, eats too much honey, and gets wedged in the doorway.
stories.description: One of the funniest chapters in all of children's literature. Pooh squeezes into Rabbit's front door, is offered honey and condensed milk, and eats so much that he cannot squeeze back out. He stays stuck for a week while his friends read to him and Christopher Robin uses his legs as a towel rack.
stories.authorBio: A. A. Milne (1882–1956) was an English playwright and novelist who created Winnie-the-Pooh for his son Christopher Robin.
stories.relevance: A perfectly self-contained bedtime story with a gentle moral about moderation, wrapped in pure comedy.

series.name: Winnie-the-Pooh
series.nextTeaser: Next: Pooh and Piglet go hunting and nearly catch a Woozle.

stories.coverScene: A small, round bear in a red shirt wedged tight in the round front door of an underground house, his bottom sticking out on one side and his head and arms on the other. A rabbit inside the burrow pushes against the bear's head. A boy in shorts reads a book to the bear's protruding backside. Sunny day, wildflowers around the door hole.

stories.title: Winnie-the-Pooh: Eeyore Has a Birthday and Gets Two Presents
stories.author: A. A. Milne
stories.summary: Pooh and Piglet each bring Eeyore a birthday present, but things don't go quite as planned.
stories.description: Eeyore's birthday is forgotten by everyone — until Pooh decides to give him a pot of honey and Piglet brings a balloon. Through a series of gentle mishaps, the honey gets eaten on the way and the balloon pops, but Eeyore is delighted anyway because an empty pot is perfect for putting a burst balloon in and out of. A tender, funny story about the thought behind a gift.
stories.authorBio: A. A. Milne (1882–1956) was an English playwright and novelist who created Winnie-the-Pooh for his son Christopher Robin.
stories.relevance: This chapter teaches children that gifts don't have to be perfect — it's the caring that counts. Perfect wind-down material.

series.name: Winnie-the-Pooh
series.nextTeaser: Next: Kanga and Baby Roo come to the Forest, and Piglet has a bath.

stories.coverScene: A grey donkey sitting alone by a stream, looking at two wrapped presents — an empty honey pot and a burst red balloon — with an expression of quiet, unexpected happiness. A small bear in a red shirt and a tiny pink piglet watch hopefully. Thistles growing by the stream. Soft, golden late-afternoon light in the Hundred Acre Wood.

stories.title: A Visit from St. Nicholas (The Night Before Christmas)
stories.author: Clement Clarke Moore
stories.summary: On Christmas Eve, a father sees Santa Claus arrive by sleigh, fill the stockings, and fly away.
stories.description: The poem that invented modern Christmas imagery — the miniature sleigh, eight tiny reindeer, Santa coming down the chimney. Moore's 1823 poem is only 56 lines long but it shaped how the Western world pictures Christmas. This LibriVox recording includes multiple readers, each bringing their own warmth to the familiar verses.
stories.authorBio: Clement Clarke Moore (1779–1863) was an American scholar and professor who wrote 'A Visit from St. Nicholas' in 1822, reportedly for his children. The poem was published anonymously the following year and became the most famous Christmas poem ever written.
stories.relevance: The ultimate Christmas Eve bedtime listen. Short enough to play twice, familiar enough that children begin reciting along.
stories.coverScene: A round, jolly man in a red fur-trimmed suit standing in front of a fireplace, a sack of toys slung over his shoulder, one finger laid aside of his nose, about to disappear up the chimney. Stockings hang from the mantel. A Christmas tree glows in the corner. Through the window, a tiny sleigh and eight reindeer silhouetted against the full moon. Warm firelight, Christmas Eve magic.

stories.title: The Canterville Ghost
stories.author: Oscar Wilde
stories.summary: An American family buys a haunted English manor and refuses to be frightened — much to the ghost's frustration.
stories.description: The Otis family moves into Canterville Chase, complete with a 300-year-old ghost. But they are practical Americans — they offer the ghost lubricant for his chains and stain remover for the bloodstain on the carpet. The ghost is humiliated. Only young Virginia Otis takes him seriously, and what she gives him in the end is not fear but something far more valuable. Funny, touching, and very Wilde.
stories.authorBio: Oscar Wilde (1854–1900) was an Irish poet, playwright, and storyteller who was received into the Catholic Church on his deathbed. His fairy tales — written for his own sons — are among the most beautiful moral stories in the English language, shot through with themes of sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness.
stories.relevance: A ghost story that is not remotely scary — funny and gentle and perfect for children who think they want to be frightened but really don't.
stories.coverScene: An American family — a father in a top hat, a mother in a bustle dress, and their children — standing in the entrance hall of a gloomy English manor, staring at a dark red stain on the stone floor. A faint ghostly figure in chains hovers behind a suit of armor in the shadows. Cobwebs, dusty portraits, candlelight. The family looks more annoyed than frightened.

stories.title: The Devoted Friend
stories.author: Oscar Wilde
stories.summary: Little Hans gives and gives to his friend the Miller, who talks about friendship but never returns it.
stories.description: A fable about the difference between talking about friendship and practicing it. The Miller visits Hans every day, eats his food, takes his flowers, and lectures him about the duties of friendship — while Hans grows poorer and poorer. Wilde's wit makes it funny; the ending makes it serious.
stories.authorBio: Oscar Wilde (1854–1900) was an Irish poet, playwright, and storyteller who was received into the Catholic Church on his deathbed. His fairy tales — written for his own sons — are among the most beautiful moral stories in the English language, shot through with themes of sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness.
stories.relevance: Wilde's fairy tales are among the most beautiful moral stories ever written — sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness, told with a poet's ear.
stories.coverScene: A small, round-faced man in a patched coat digging in a garden on a cold morning, looking up with a trusting smile at a large, well-dressed man leaning on the garden gate who waves cheerfully but makes no move to help. A wheelbarrow full of flowers between them. The gardener's cottage is tiny and bare; the other man's house looms large behind the hedge. Frosty morning light.

stories.title: The Happy Prince
stories.author: Oscar Wilde
stories.summary: A golden statue and a little swallow give away everything they have to help the poor of the city.
stories.description: Wilde's most famous fairy tale. A statue covered in gold leaf and jewels watches the suffering of the city below and asks a migrating swallow to strip away his beauty piece by piece and give it to the poor. The swallow stays too long and the ending is not happy in any ordinary sense — but it is holy. One of the great stories about charity in the English language.
stories.authorBio: Oscar Wilde (1854–1900) was an Irish poet, playwright, and storyteller who was received into the Catholic Church on his deathbed. His fairy tales — written for his own sons — are among the most beautiful moral stories in the English language, shot through with themes of sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness.
stories.relevance: Wilde's fairy tales are among the most beautiful moral stories ever written — sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness, told with a poet's ear.
stories.coverScene: A golden statue of a prince on a tall column in a city square at night, a small brown swallow perched on his shoulder. The prince's sapphire eyes are weeping. Below, narrow streets of a poor city — a seamstress sewing by candlelight in a garret window, a match girl shivering in a doorway. The statue gleams in moonlight while the city below is dark.

stories.title: The Nightingale and the Rose
stories.author: Oscar Wilde
stories.summary: A nightingale gives her life to create a red rose for a young student in love.
stories.description: A student needs a red rose to win a girl's heart, and a nightingale sings all night with a thorn pressed against her breast until a white rose turns red with her blood. It is a story about the cost of love and the blindness of those who don't understand it. Beautiful and painful — better for older children.
stories.authorBio: Oscar Wilde (1854–1900) was an Irish poet, playwright, and storyteller who was received into the Catholic Church on his deathbed. His fairy tales — written for his own sons — are among the most beautiful moral stories in the English language, shot through with themes of sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness.
stories.relevance: Wilde's fairy tales are among the most beautiful moral stories ever written — sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness, told with a poet's ear.
stories.coverScene: A small brown nightingale pressing her breast against the thorn of a white rose bush in a moonlit garden, singing. A single drop of red spreads from the thorn into the rose, turning its petals crimson. In the background, a young student reads at a desk by candlelight in a window. Intense moonlight, the garden silent except for the song.

stories.title: The Remarkable Rocket
stories.author: Oscar Wilde
stories.summary: A firework at a royal wedding is so impressed with himself that he fails to go off at the right moment.
stories.description: Wilde's funniest fairy tale — a satire on vanity in the form of a self-important rocket who talks endlessly about his own greatness while sputtering in a ditch. Children enjoy the absurdity; adults recognize the type.
stories.authorBio: Oscar Wilde (1854–1900) was an Irish poet, playwright, and storyteller who was received into the Catholic Church on his deathbed. His fairy tales — written for his own sons — are among the most beautiful moral stories in the English language, shot through with themes of sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness.
stories.relevance: Wilde's fairy tales are among the most beautiful moral stories ever written — sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness, told with a poet's ear.
stories.coverScene: A tall, ornate firework rocket standing upright in a garden shed, its painted surface gleaming with gold and red stars, looking down its nose at a collection of smaller, humbler fireworks — a Catherine wheel, a Roman candle, and a squib — arranged around it. A garden party is visible through the shed door. Warm afternoon light, festive bunting.

stories.title: The Selfish Giant
stories.author: Oscar Wilde
stories.summary: A giant builds a wall around his garden and discovers that without children, it is always winter.
stories.description: Wilde's shortest and most explicitly Christian fairy tale. A giant forbids children from his garden; the garden freezes. When he relents and lets them back in, spring returns. The ending — with a small child whose hands bear the marks of nails — is Wilde at his most reverent. A perfect bedtime story for any age.
stories.authorBio: Oscar Wilde (1854–1900) was an Irish poet, playwright, and storyteller who was received into the Catholic Church on his deathbed. His fairy tales — written for his own sons — are among the most beautiful moral stories in the English language, shot through with themes of sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness.
stories.relevance: Wilde's fairy tales are among the most beautiful moral stories ever written — sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness, told with a poet's ear.
stories.coverScene: A giant in a fur cloak standing in a walled garden that is frozen in perpetual winter — bare trees, snow on the ground, frost on the wall — while outside the wall, spring blooms in every other garden. A small child has climbed through a crack in the wall and stands beneath a bare tree that is beginning to blossom where the child touches it. The giant watches, his hard face softening.

stories.title: The Star-Child
stories.author: Oscar Wilde
stories.summary: A beautiful boy found in the snow is cruel to everyone he meets — until he is cursed to wear the face he deserves.
stories.description: A child falls from the stars and is raised by a woodcutter. He grows beautiful and proud and mocks the ugly and the poor. When his real mother — a beggar woman — finds him, he rejects her and is cursed to become as ugly outside as he is within. The journey back is long. Wilde's most explicit parable of repentance.
stories.authorBio: Oscar Wilde (1854–1900) was an Irish poet, playwright, and storyteller who was received into the Catholic Church on his deathbed. His fairy tales — written for his own sons — are among the most beautiful moral stories in the English language, shot through with themes of sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness.
stories.relevance: Wilde's deeper fairy tales — for older children who are ready for stories about the cost of cruelty and the possibility of redemption.
stories.coverScene: A beautiful boy with golden hair standing in a forest clearing, looking at his own reflection in a pool. His face is perfect but his expression is cruel. Behind him, an old beggar woman in rags reaches toward him with a pleading hand. The pool reflects the boy's beauty but also, faintly, the shape of a toad. Cold, bright, fairytale light.

stories.title: The Young King
stories.author: Oscar Wilde
stories.summary: A young king dreams of the suffering behind his coronation robes and refuses to wear them.
stories.description: On the night before his coronation, a young king has three dreams showing him the human cost of his luxury — pearl divers drowning, weavers starving, miners dying. He goes to his crowning in a goatherd's cloak. The court is horrified; God is not. Wilde's most explicitly social-justice fairy tale.
stories.authorBio: Oscar Wilde (1854–1900) was an Irish poet, playwright, and storyteller who was received into the Catholic Church on his deathbed. His fairy tales — written for his own sons — are among the most beautiful moral stories in the English language, shot through with themes of sacrifice, compassion, and the cost of selfishness.
stories.relevance: Wilde's deeper fairy tales — for older children who are ready for stories about the cost of cruelty and the possibility of redemption.
stories.coverScene: A boy in a rough shepherd's tunic standing before a throne in a cathedral, refusing a golden crown held out to him by a bishop. Sunlight streams through a stained-glass window and falls on the boy, who wears a crown of thorns made of briar and holds a rough wooden staff instead of a scepter. The court looks scandalized. The boy is radiant.

stories.title: A Child's Garden of Verses
stories.author: Robert Louis Stevenson
stories.summary: Poems about the world as a child sees it — the land of counterpane, the lamplighter, the shadow that goes in and out, and the swing that goes up so high you can see over the garden wall.
stories.description: Stevenson's collection of poems for children, published in 1885. These are not poems about childhood written by an adult looking back — they are poems written from inside a child's experience. The bed is a ship, the garden is a kingdom, and the shadow on the nursery wall is a mysterious companion.
stories.authorBio: Robert Louis Stevenson (1850–1894) was a Scottish novelist, poet, and travel writer. A Child's Garden of Verses, published in 1885, captures the world as seen by a child — shadows on the wall, the lamplighter, the land of counterpane. It is one of the most enduring poetry collections for children.
stories.relevance: Among the finest children's poetry ever written. Each poem is short enough for bedtime, musical enough to memorize, and true enough to the way children actually see the world.
stories.coverScene: A small child in a nightgown standing on tiptoe at a bedroom window, looking out at a lamplighter making his way down a cobblestone street, touching his long pole to each gas lamp, which blooms into golden light one by one. The child's room is warm with a fire; the street outside is blue dusk. Rooftops and chimneys against a fading sky.

stories.title: Treasure Island
stories.author: Robert Louis Stevenson
stories.summary: An old sea captain with a sabre cut across his cheek takes a room at the Admiral Benbow inn — and Jim Hawkins's life changes forever.
stories.description: The first chapter of the greatest adventure story in the English language. Billy Bones arrives at the inn, drinks rum, watches the sea, and terrifies everyone. The blind man is coming. The black spot is coming. The treasure map is hidden in the sea-chest. Stevenson wrote it to be read aloud, chapter by chapter, and it has never lost that power.
stories.authorBio: Robert Louis Stevenson (1850–1894) was a Scottish novelist, poet, and travel writer who wrote Treasure Island, Kidnapped, and A Child's Garden of Verses. He drew the map of Treasure Island for his stepson and then wrote the story to go with it.
stories.relevance: A chapter-a-night book — the kind that makes bedtime something to look forward to.
stories.coverScene: A boy in a rough coat standing behind the bar of a seaside inn, staring at a tall, weather-beaten man with a saber scar across his cheek who has just pushed through the door, a sea chest dragged behind him. Rain lashes the windows. A fire crackles in the hearth. The man's coat drips salt water. The boy grips the counter nervously. Grey coastal storm light.

stories.title: How the Camel Got His Hump
stories.author: Rudyard Kipling
stories.summary: The Camel says 'Humph!' when asked to work — and the Djinn gives him a hump to work off.
stories.description: From Just So Stories by Rudyard Kipling.
stories.authorBio: Rudyard Kipling (1865–1936) was a British author and poet born in Bombay during the Raj. He wrote The Jungle Book, Kim, and Just So Stories, and in 1907 became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature. His best work for children carries the warmth of stories invented at bedtime for his own family.
stories.relevance: Part of the beloved Just So Stories — perfect for chapter-a-night bedtime reading.
stories.coverScene: A camel sitting stubbornly in the middle of a flat desert, refusing to move, while a horse, a dog, and an ox stand around it looking exasperated. A djinn made of swirling golden sand hovers behind the camel, one finger pointed at the camel's back. The desert stretches flat to the horizon. Blazing noon sun, shimmering heat.

stories.title: How the Rhinoceros Got His Skin
stories.author: Rudyard Kipling
stories.summary: The Parsee bakes a cake, the Rhinoceros eats it, and the Parsee gets his revenge with cake crumbs.
stories.description: From Just So Stories by Rudyard Kipling.
stories.authorBio: Rudyard Kipling (1865–1936) was a British author and poet born in Bombay during the Raj. He wrote The Jungle Book, Kim, and Just So Stories, and in 1907 became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature. His best work for children carries the warmth of stories invented at bedtime for his own family.
stories.relevance: Part of the beloved Just So Stories — perfect for chapter-a-night bedtime reading.
stories.coverScene: A man in a pith helmet and white suit standing on a tropical beach beside a cooking stove, holding up a cake while a rhinoceros — smooth-skinned, without wrinkles — watches hungrily from behind a palm tree. A red volcanic island rises from the sea behind them. Bright tropical light, turquoise water.

stories.title: How the Whale Got His Throat
stories.author: Rudyard Kipling
stories.summary: The first of the Just So Stories — how a clever sailor and a tiny grate gave the whale his peculiar throat.
stories.description: The opening story of Kipling's Just So Stories, written for his daughter Josephine. A great whale eats every fish in the sea, then a single shipwrecked mariner — 'a man of infinite-resource-and-sagacity' — and once inside, he stomps and dances until the whale agrees to set him on the shore. The mariner leaves a small wooden grate behind in the whale's throat, which is why, to this day, whales eat only the very tiniest of fishes. Kipling's prose has the lilt of a story told aloud, with repeated phrases that beg to be read in a rhythm.
stories.authorBio: Rudyard Kipling (1865–1936) was a British author and poet born in Bombay during the Raj. He wrote The Jungle Book, Kim, and Just So Stories, and in 1907 became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature. His best work for children carries the warmth of stories invented at bedtime for his own family.
stories.relevance: The Just So Stories were composed to be read aloud. The cadence, the made-up words, the asides to 'O Best Beloved' — all of it is built for a parent's voice. Reading them is genuinely good practice for everything else.

series.name: Just So Stories
series.nextTeaser: How the Camel Got His Hump — and what happens to creatures who say 'Humph!' when they should be working.

stories.coverScene: An enormous whale with its mouth wide open in a grey-green sea, and inside its mouth a tiny man sitting on a raft made of his own suspenders and a jackknife, looking remarkably calm. The whale's eye is enormous and startled. Waves and seabirds around them. Cold northern ocean light, vast and humorous scale.

stories.title: Just So Stories
stories.author: Rudyard Kipling
stories.summary: A young elephant full of 'satiable curiosity goes to the Limpopo River to find out what the Crocodile has for dinner.
stories.description: From Just So Stories by Rudyard Kipling.
stories.authorBio: Rudyard Kipling (1865–1936) was a British author and poet born in Bombay during the Raj. He wrote The Jungle Book, Kim, and Just So Stories, and in 1907 became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature. His best work for children carries the warmth of stories invented at bedtime for his own family.
stories.relevance: Part of the beloved Just So Stories — perfect for chapter-a-night bedtime reading.
stories.coverScene: A young elephant with a short, bulgy nose leaning over the edge of a grey-green river, nose-to-nose with a crocodile whose jaws are clamped around the elephant's nose and pulling it longer. The elephant braces its hind legs in the mud. A snake watches from a tree branch. African riverside, warm golden light, dramatic tug-of-war.

stories.title: The Jungle Book
stories.author: Rudyard Kipling
stories.summary: Mowgli is kidnapped by the Bandar-log — the foolish, lawless monkeys — and carried deep into the ruins of an ancient city. Baloo and Bagheera must call on Kaa the great python to rescue him.
stories.description: The third Mowgli story from The Jungle Book. Kaa's Hunting introduces the danger of those who have no law and no discipline — the Bandar-log, who promise everything and finish nothing. It is also the story where Mowgli learns just how far his friends will go to protect him.
stories.authorBio: Rudyard Kipling (1865–1936) was a British author and poet born in Bombay during the Raj. He wrote The Jungle Book, Kim, and Just So Stories, and in 1907 became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature.
stories.relevance: An exciting rescue adventure with a clear moral: the monkey-people are dangerous precisely because they have no rules and no loyalty. The contrast between the disciplined jungle and the lawless monkeys teaches without preaching.
stories.coverScene: A brown boy in a loincloth carried high above the jungle canopy by a troop of grey monkeys swinging through the treetops, their arms linked in a chain. Below, a great black panther races along a jungle trail, and an enormous python coils through the branches in pursuit. Dense Indian jungle, vines, and filtered green light.

stories.title: The Jungle Book
stories.author: Rudyard Kipling
stories.summary: Mowgli's Brothers — a man-cub is taken in by a family of wolves on the night of a Council in the hills.
stories.description: Chapter 1 — 'Mowgli's Brothers.' Father Wolf is just waking up for his evening's hunting on the Seeonee hills when a small, naked, brown baby crawls into the cave, looks up at him, and laughs. Shere Khan the tiger comes prowling for the child, but Mother Wolf — Raksha, 'the Demon' — refuses to give him up. The story moves to the Council Rock, where the law of the pack must decide whether the man-cub may live among the wolves. It's a wild, formal, ceremonious chapter, full of names that ring like an incantation: Akela, Bagheera, Baloo.
stories.authorBio: Rudyard Kipling (1865–1936) was born in Bombay and spent his early childhood in India before being sent to England for school. He wrote The Jungle Book in Vermont in 1894, drawing on the India of his memory. The stories of Mowgli have shaped how generations have imagined the jungle, for better and for worse — they reward reading with a parent's eye.
stories.relevance: Kipling at his most cinematic. The chapter has clear stakes, fierce love between Mother Wolf and Mowgli, and a long ceremonial scene that reads beautifully aloud. Better for slightly older children — there is danger, and a tiger.

series.name: The Jungle Book
series.nextTeaser: Baloo the bear and Bagheera the panther begin teaching Mowgli the Law of the Jungle — and Mowgli is kidnapped by the Bandar-log.

stories.coverScene: A small brown boy standing barefoot among a circle of wolves in a moonlit jungle clearing, one hand resting on the head of a great grey wolf. A black panther watches from a low branch. Yellow eyes gleam from the darkness at the edges of the clearing. The boy is small but unafraid. Full moon, silver light on dark jungle.

stories.title: The Three Bears (Steel's English Fairy Tales)
stories.author: Flora Annie Steel
stories.summary: Goldilocks wanders into the house of three bears and tries their porridge, chairs, and beds.
stories.description: Flora Annie Steel's retelling of the Three Bears is the version most of us grew up with — lively, rhythmic, and perfectly structured for reading aloud. The repeated pattern of 'too hot, too cold, just right' is one of the first narrative structures children learn to anticipate, and the ending sends Goldilocks running home with a fright but no real harm done.
stories.authorBio: Flora Annie Steel (1847–1929) was a British author who spent many years in India collecting folklore. Her English Fairy Tales (1918) became one of the definitive collections, distinguished by her clean, vigorous prose style.
stories.relevance: Steel's fairy tales are specifically written to be read aloud. Her prose is more rhythmic and dramatic than Jacobs, making these ideal audio stories.
stories.coverScene: Three bears — a great big bear, a middle-sized bear, and a tiny wee bear — standing in their cottage kitchen, staring at three bowls of porridge on the table. The biggest bowl is untouched, the middle bowl has a spoon in it, and the smallest bowl is empty. Through the doorway, golden curls are just visible disappearing up the stairs. Cozy cottage interior, morning light.

stories.title: Jack and the Beanstalk (Steel's English Fairy Tales)
stories.author: Flora Annie Steel
stories.summary: Jack trades a cow for magic beans, climbs a beanstalk into the clouds, and outwits a giant.
stories.description: Steel's Jack and the Beanstalk has all the vigor of the oral tradition — Fee-fi-fo-fum delivered with genuine menace, Jack's quick thinking in the giant's kitchen, and the triumphant chop of the beanstalk at the end. It's a story about daring, cleverness, and the rewards of taking risks.
stories.authorBio: Flora Annie Steel (1847–1929) was a British author whose English Fairy Tales (1918) became one of the definitive collections of English folklore.
stories.relevance: One of the most requested bedtime stories ever. Steel's version has the best pacing for audio — crisp, dramatic, and perfectly timed.
stories.coverScene: A boy in patched trousers standing at the base of an enormous beanstalk, craning his neck to look up. The beanstalk is thick as an oak, its broad leaves spiraling upward into the clouds. In his hand he holds a small leather pouch that jingles with gold coins. His expression is triumphant but breathless. Morning mist, the beanstalk vanishing into bright cloud.

stories.title: Old Mother West Wind
stories.author: Thornton W. Burgess
stories.summary: Another morning on the Green Meadows — Old Mother West Wind opens her big bag and lets out all the Merry Little Breezes to play, while the animals of the meadow have adventures of their own.
stories.description: The second chapter of Burgess's first book. Old Mother West Wind arrives each morning to let her children — the Merry Little Breezes — out to play. The stories are gentle nature tales with clear morals, set in a world of talking animals that feels entirely real to a three-year-old.
stories.authorBio: Thornton W. Burgess (1874–1965) was an American conservationist and author who wrote over 170 books and 15,000 stories about the animals of the Green Meadows and the Green Forest. His bedtime stories — originally written for his own son — teach kindness to animals and respect for nature.
stories.relevance: Perfect nature stories for the youngest listeners. Each chapter is short, gentle, and teaches a simple lesson about honesty, sharing, or kindness — wrapped in the daily life of the Green Meadows.
stories.coverScene: A mischievous young rabbit sitting at the edge of the Smiling Pool, dangling his feet in the water and chatting with a round green frog on a lily pad. A kingfisher perches on a cattail nearby. The Green Meadows stretch behind them, dotted with daisies. Warm mid-morning light, everything green and cheerful.

stories.title: Old Mother West Wind
stories.author: Thornton W. Burgess
stories.summary: Old Mother West Wind comes down from the Purple Hills and lets her Merry Little Breezes out to play on the Green Meadows.
stories.description: From Old Mother West Wind — stories about the natural world told with reverence and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Thornton W. Burgess (1874–1965) was an American naturalist and children's author who wrote over 170 books and 15,000 stories, most featuring the animals of the Green Meadows and Green Forest. His stories teach honesty, kindness, and respect for nature.
stories.relevance: Nature stories for bedtime — because the world outside the window is as full of stories as any fairy tale.
stories.coverScene: A plump, kindly woman made of wind and cloud descending from the sky into a green meadow at dawn, opening a large bag from which tumble dozens of tiny, laughing breeze-children who scatter in every direction through the wildflowers. A rabbit and a chipmunk watch from a stone wall. Pink sunrise sky, dew on every blade of grass.

stories.title: The Adventures of Buster Bear
stories.author: Thornton W. Burgess
stories.summary: Buster Bear arrives in the Green Forest — and everyone is afraid of him, except Farmer Brown's boy.
stories.description: From The Adventures of Buster Bear — stories about the natural world told with reverence and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Thornton W. Burgess's Buster Bear stories follow a young bear who has just moved to the Green Forest. The other animals are afraid of him — but Buster is more afraid of Farmer Brown's boy.
stories.relevance: Nature stories for bedtime — because the world outside the window is as full of stories as any fairy tale.
stories.coverScene: A large brown bear cub standing on his hind legs at the edge of a blueberry patch, paws purple with juice, looking guiltily over his shoulder at his mother who approaches through the underbrush. A chipmunk on a nearby rock chatters at the cub. Summer forest, dappled light, ripe berries everywhere.

stories.title: The Adventures of Reddy Fox
stories.author: Thornton W. Burgess
stories.summary: Reddy Fox is too clever for his own good — and Granny Fox has to teach him a lesson about showing off.
stories.description: From The Adventures of Reddy Fox — stories about the natural world told with reverence and wonder.
stories.authorBio: Thornton W. Burgess (1874–1965) wrote stories about animals with real personalities — Reddy Fox is vain and clever, and his adventures teach children about consequences.
stories.relevance: Nature stories for bedtime — because the world outside the window is as full of stories as any fairy tale.
stories.coverScene: A young red fox with a bushy tail trotting along a stone wall at the edge of a farm, pausing to sniff the air, ears pricked forward. In the farmyard beyond, a plump hen scratches in the dirt, unaware. A farmer's scarecrow stands in the garden. Early morning, ground mist, golden light on the fox's red fur.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: The Flight
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: Four orphaned children run away and begin searching for a place to call home.
stories.description: Henry, Jessie, Violet, and Benny Alden are orphaned and on the run. They've heard their grandfather is mean, so they set off on their own. In this opening chapter, the children's resourcefulness and loyalty to each other shine through as they navigate a world that seems too big for them. Warner wrote the original in 1924 to show that children could be both self-reliant and kind.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: The children spend a cold, hungry night and decide they must find shelter.

stories.coverScene: Four children walking single-file along a railroad track at dusk — the oldest boy carrying the youngest child on his back, the oldest girl with dark hair carrying a bundle, and a younger girl holding a cracked teacup. An empty red boxcar sits on a siding in the distance. Warm fading light, tall grass on either side of the tracks.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: The Race
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: Henry enters a local footrace and the whole family cheers him on.
stories.description: Henry enters a Fourth of July footrace in town. His siblings watch nervously from the crowd as he competes against older, bigger boys. The race is exciting, but what matters most is the family's pride in each other — win or lose.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: More Education.

stories.coverScene: A teenage boy in a running vest sprinting along a dusty race course toward a finish-line ribbon, arms pumping. In the spectator area, three children — two girls and a small boy — lean forward and cheer, the smallest boy standing on a wooden crate to see. An American flag flutters from a pole. Fourth of July, bright midday light.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: More Education
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: The children find creative ways to keep learning even without school.
stories.description: Jessie worries that the children are falling behind in their education. They devise their own lessons — Henry teaches from a newspaper, Jessie practices arithmetic with their grocery money, and even Benny learns his letters. Warner gently shows that curiosity and determination matter more than a classroom.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: Ginseng.

stories.coverScene: Four children sitting in a circle inside their red boxcar, each focused on a lesson — the oldest boy reading aloud from a newspaper, the oldest girl with dark hair writing numbers on a slate, the younger girl tracing letters, and the smallest boy counting on his fingers. A brown dog sleeps across the doorway. Quiet afternoon study session, sunlight on the boxcar floor.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: Ginseng
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: The children discover a valuable wild plant and learn about the treasures hidden in nature.
stories.description: While exploring the woods, the children find ginseng — a plant worth real money. Henry researches it and they carefully harvest and dry it. The discovery adds to their small income and teaches them to pay attention to the natural world around them.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: Trouble.

stories.coverScene: Two children crouching in a forest clearing, carefully digging up a plant with forked roots from the dark earth. The oldest boy holds the plant up while the younger girl examines the roots closely. Dappled green light, ferns and mossy logs around them, a sense of quiet discovery in the deep woods.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: Trouble
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: Violet falls seriously ill and the children face their biggest crisis yet.
stories.description: Violet becomes dangerously sick with a fever. The other children are terrified — they have no medicine, no doctor, and calling for help might mean being found and separated. Henry must make a difficult choice between keeping their secret and saving his sister. The stakes have never been higher.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: Caught.

stories.coverScene: The oldest boy with a worried face running down a forest trail at full speed, glancing back at a red boxcar where the oldest girl with dark hair sits on the doorstep holding a limp, feverish younger girl wrapped in a blanket. The smallest boy stands nearby looking frightened. Overcast sky, urgency in the boy's stride.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: Caught
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: Dr. Moore discovers the children's secret and brings help — but also brings change.
stories.description: With Violet gravely ill, Henry runs to Dr. Moore for help. The doctor comes to the boxcar, and the children's carefully guarded secret is finally out. But Dr. Moore is kind, not threatening, and his concern for the children's wellbeing is genuine. Change is coming whether they want it or not.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
