stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: A New Grandfather.

stories.coverScene: A kind-looking man in a white coat kneeling inside a red boxcar, taking the pulse of a pale girl who lies on a pine-needle bed. The other three children watch anxiously from the doorway. The man's face is gentle and reassuring. Afternoon light slanting through the boxcar door, the stethoscope around his neck catching the light.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: A New Grandfather
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: The children discover that the grandfather they feared is nothing like they imagined.
stories.description: Dr. Moore reveals a stunning truth — the kind man who has been helping Henry with work is actually their grandfather, James Alden. He has been searching for them all along, and he is nothing like the mean old man they were told about. The children's fear melts into wonder.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: A United Family.

stories.coverScene: Four children standing in a grand entrance hall, mouths open in astonishment, as a white-haired gentleman in a fine suit smiles warmly at them with outstretched hands. The hall has a marble floor, a crystal chandelier, and a wide staircase. The children's clothes are patched and dusty. The contrast between the man's elegance and their poverty. Warm afternoon light through tall windows.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: A United Family
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: Grandfather Alden welcomes the children into his home and gives them a surprise they never expected.
stories.description: James Alden brings the children to his large, comfortable house. He has prepared rooms for each of them, and he even has their beloved boxcar moved to his backyard so they can play in it whenever they want. The family is finally united, and the children realize that home isn't a place — it's the people who love you.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: Safe.

stories.coverScene: Four children exploring a beautiful bedroom in a grand house — each child touching something in wonder: a real bed, a bookshelf, a window seat, a toy chest. Through the window, a familiar red boxcar sits in the backyard garden on the grass. The smallest boy presses his face to the window, pointing at the boxcar and laughing. Golden light, new home, old home visible outside.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: Safe
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: The Alden children settle into their new life, finally safe and together.
stories.description: The final chapter wraps the story in warmth. The children explore their new home, meet their grandfather's household, and begin to understand that being found was not the end of their adventure but the beginning of a better one. Benny insists on sleeping in the boxcar one more time, and Grandfather smiles and lets him. Warner closes with the quiet reassurance that every child deserves — you are safe, you are loved, and you belong.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.
stories.coverScene: Four children and a white-haired gentleman sitting together on a porch swing in the evening, a brown dog at their feet. The smallest boy sleeps against the old man's arm. The oldest girl with dark hair leans on the railing, looking at the stars. Fireflies in the garden. The red boxcar just visible in moonlight at the edge of the lawn. Peace, safety, home at last.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: The Second Night
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: The four children spend a cold, hungry night in a bakery doorway and decide they must find shelter.
stories.description: After fleeing on their own, the Alden children huddle together through a difficult second night. Jessie takes charge, comforting little Benny and keeping everyone's spirits up. Their determination to stick together rather than be separated drives them deeper into the countryside.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: Shelter.

stories.coverScene: Four children huddled together in the doorway of a closed bakery on a dark street, the oldest girl with dark hair wrapping her shawl around the youngest boy who sleeps against her shoulder. The oldest boy stands watch, peering into the darkness. A streetlamp casts a pale circle of light. Cold night, the children press close for warmth.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: Shelter
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: The children search the woods for a safe place to sleep and stumble upon something promising.
stories.description: Exhausted and hungry, the children push through the forest looking for any kind of shelter. Henry scouts ahead while Jessie keeps Benny and Violet close. The simple act of finding a dry, safe spot becomes a small triumph — a reminder that even in hard times, looking out for each other is enough.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: The children discover an abandoned boxcar in the woods and turn it into their home.

stories.coverScene: Four children in simple 1920s clothes pushing through thick underbrush in a forest, the oldest boy holding back branches for the others. The younger girl looks tired; the smallest boy rides on his brother's back. Shafts of light break through the canopy ahead, suggesting a clearing. Late afternoon, forest shadows.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: A New Home
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: The children discover an abandoned boxcar in the woods and turn it into their home.
stories.description: Deep in the woods, the Alden children find an old red boxcar. With nothing but their wits and each other, they transform it into a cozy home — sweeping the floor, gathering pine needles for beds, and finding a cracked cup in a dump that becomes their most prized possession. It's a chapter about making something beautiful out of nothing.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924.
stories.relevance: Children love the fantasy of building their own home. This chapter is imaginative, resourceful, and deeply satisfying.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: The children organize their boxcar home with clever solutions.

stories.coverScene: Four children peering inside an old red boxcar with its sliding door half open, their faces lit with excitement and relief. The oldest girl with dark hair steps up onto the boxcar floor. Inside, a wooden crate serves as a table. The boxcar sits in a clearing among pines. Golden afternoon light flooding the interior through the open door.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: Housekeeping
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: The children organize their boxcar home, finding clever solutions for everyday needs.
stories.description: With their boxcar claimed, the Aldens set about making it livable. They gather blueberries for breakfast, find a dump where they salvage dishes and a tablecloth, and divide up chores. Violet arranges wildflowers. Benny names their dog Watch. The chapter is a masterclass in resourcefulness and the joy of making a home from nothing.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: Earning a Living.

stories.coverScene: Four children inside a red boxcar turned into a cozy home — the oldest girl sweeping the floor with a branch broom, the younger girl arranging wildflowers in a cracked cup on a crate-table, the smallest boy setting out mismatched dishes, the oldest boy carrying an armload of pine branches for bedding. Warm light through the open door, a sense of pride and order.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: Earning a Living
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: Henry finds work to support his siblings while keeping their hideout a secret.
stories.description: Henry walks to town and finds a job mowing lawns for a kind doctor named Dr. Moore. He works hard all day and brings home wages and food, carefully hiding where he lives. The other children contribute too — picking berries to sell. Warner shows children that honest work and family loyalty go hand in hand.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: At Home.

stories.coverScene: A teenage boy pushing a lawn mower across a tidy suburban lawn while a kind-looking man in a white coat watches from a porch. The boy wipes sweat from his forehead. In his pocket, folded dollar bills are just visible. In the distance, a forest hides a red boxcar among the pines. Bright summer day, hard work.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: At Home
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: Life in the boxcar settles into a happy routine as the children build their little world.
stories.description: The Aldens have found their rhythm. They swim in the brook, cook simple meals over a fire, and take care of each other with surprising competence. Benny's cheerful commentary provides constant humor. The chapter captures the dream every child has — living independently in the woods with the people you love most.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

series.name: The Boxcar Children
series.nextTeaser: Next: Building the Dam.

stories.coverScene: Four children sitting around a small campfire beside a red boxcar at twilight, the oldest girl stirring a pot while the smallest boy feeds sticks to the fire and a dog — a brown mutt — lies beside him. The younger girl reads from a book. A brook glistens nearby. Firelight on their faces, contentment, the forest settling into evening.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: Building the Dam
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: The children build a swimming pool by damming their brook — and discover they make a great team.
stories.description: Henry and Jessie lead a project to dam the brook and create a proper swimming hole. Everyone contributes — even little Benny hauls stones. The engineering is simple but the teamwork is genuine, and by the end they have a cool, clear pool in the summer heat.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

stories.coverScene: Four children in rolled-up trousers and bare feet building a dam of stones across a shallow brook, water splashing around their legs. The oldest boy places a large rock while the smallest boy carries one half his size. Dappled forest light, the brook pooling behind the growing dam, summer heat.

stories.title: The Boxcar Children: Cherry Picking
stories.author: Gertrude Chandler Warner
stories.summary: The children earn money picking cherries and Benny charms everyone he meets.
stories.description: Dr. Moore arranges for all four children to help pick cherries at a nearby farm. Benny's enthusiastic personality wins over the farmer, and Violet's quiet helpfulness earns respect. The children prove they can work alongside adults while keeping their secret safe.
stories.authorBio: Gertrude Chandler Warner (1890–1979) was an American teacher and author who wrote The Boxcar Children in 1924. The series eventually grew to over 150 books and remains one of the best-selling children's series of all time.
stories.relevance: A story about siblings who stick together no matter what — exactly the kind of courage and warmth that makes a good bedtime adventure.

stories.coverScene: Four children standing in a cherry orchard, the trees heavy with bright red fruit. The oldest boy picks from a high branch, the oldest girl with dark hair fills a basket, the younger girl reaches for a low cluster, and the smallest boy has cherry juice smeared across his grinning face. A farmer watches approvingly from a ladder. Bright summer morning.

stories.title: West African Folk Tales
stories.author: William H. Barker
stories.summary: Anansi the spider spins a tale — and a trick — in the forests of West Africa.
stories.description: From West African Folk Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: William H. Barker collected these Ashanti and West African folk tales with Cecilia Sinclair. The collection features Anansi the spider-trickster and other characters from the rich storytelling tradition of West Africa.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.
stories.coverScene: Anansi the Spider — a large, clever-looking spider with bright eyes — sitting at the center of a web strung between two palm trees, gesturing with several legs as he speaks to a crowd of forest animals gathered below: a tortoise, a leopard, a parrot, and a hornbill. West African forest clearing, warm golden light through tropical canopy.

stories.title: American Indian Fairy Tales
stories.author: William Trowbridge Larned
stories.summary: Stories from the Great Lakes — of Manabozho the trickster and the spirits of the northern woods.
stories.description: From American Indian Fairy Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: William Trowbridge Larned retold Ojibwe stories from the Lake Superior region as children's fairy tales in 1921, drawing on the earlier collections of Henry Rowe Schoolcraft.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.
stories.coverScene: A young Native American girl sitting cross-legged by a campfire under a vast star-filled sky, listening to an elder who gestures toward the stars as he tells a story. Teepees stand behind them on a grassy plain. The Milky Way blazes overhead. The fire casts warm orange light on their faces. Wide prairie, enormous sky.

stories.title: The Velveteen Rabbit
stories.author: Margery Williams
stories.summary: A stuffed rabbit longs to become Real, and learns that love is what makes it happen.
stories.description: A boy receives a velveteen rabbit for Christmas. At first forgotten among flashier toys, the Rabbit is told by the wise Skin Horse that toys become Real when a child truly loves them. Through months of being hugged, dragged through the garden, and slept beside, the Rabbit's velveteen fur wears thin — but a kind fairy transforms him at last into a real rabbit. Williams wrote a story about the transformative power of love that has not aged a day since 1922.
stories.authorBio: Margery Williams Bianco (1881–1944) was an English-American author best known for The Velveteen Rabbit, published in 1922. She wrote over thirty books for children, but this one endures above all.
stories.relevance: A perfect bedtime story — gentle, deeply emotional, and reassuring. The idea that being loved makes you real is one children carry with them forever.
stories.coverScene: A small stuffed rabbit made of brown velveteen sitting in a nursery beside a sleeping boy, its button eyes gazing out the window at a garden where two real rabbits play in the moonlight. The stuffed rabbit's fur is worn thin, one ear droops, but its expression is full of longing. Warm nightlight inside, silver moonlight outside.

stories.title: Japanese Fairy Tales
stories.author: Yei Theodora Ozaki
stories.summary: Momotaro, the Peach Boy, floats down the river in a giant peach and grows up to fight the demons of Ogre Island.
stories.description: From Japanese Fairy Tales — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: Yei Theodora Ozaki (1869–1932) was a Japanese-English translator who collected and retold Japanese folk tales for Western readers. Her 1908 collection, prepared in consultation with Andrew Lang, remains the standard introduction to Japanese fairy tales in English.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.
stories.coverScene: A fisherman in a straw hat kneeling on the shore beside a golden turtle, gently untangling it from a net. Behind him, a fishing village with curved-roof houses. A distant palace shimmers beneath the waves — visible through the translucent water like a dream. Japanese coastal scene, mist, pine trees on the cliff, soft morning light.

stories.title: The Tongue-Cut Sparrow
stories.author: Yei Theodora Ozaki
stories.summary: A kind old man cares for an injured sparrow, but his greedy wife drives it away. When the old man follows the sparrow to its enchanted home, he is rewarded — but greed carries its own punishment.
stories.description: One of the most famous Japanese fairy tales. The Tongue-Cut Sparrow teaches that kindness to the small and helpless is rewarded, and that greed destroys. The old man chooses the small treasure box; the greedy wife demands the large one — with very different results.
stories.authorBio: Yei Theodora Ozaki (1871–1932) was an English-born translator who spent much of her life in Japan. Her Japanese Fairy Tales brought beloved stories like Momotaro and The Tongue-Cut Sparrow to English-speaking children for the first time.
stories.relevance: A beloved Japanese tale about kindness and greed. The moral is clear and vivid — the right box is always the small one, chosen with a grateful heart.
stories.coverScene: An old man in a kimono kneeling before a tiny sparrow perched on a branch, bowing to it respectfully. The sparrow wears a tiny bandage around its beak. Behind the man, a small Japanese garden with a stone lantern, raked gravel, and a gnarled pine. Soft overcast light, cherry blossoms drifting.

stories.title: Old Indian Legends
stories.author: Zitkala-Sa
stories.summary: Iktomi the trickster weaves a web of mischief in the Dakota legends.
stories.description: From Old Indian Legends — folk tales that have been teaching virtue and wonder to children for centuries.
stories.authorBio: Zitkala-Sa (1876–1938), also known as Gertrude Simmons Bonnin, was a Yankton Dakota writer, musician, and political activist. She collected and retold these legends from her own people's oral tradition — one of the first Native American women to write without a white intermediary.
stories.relevance: Folk tales from around the world — because virtue is universal and every culture has stories about it.
stories.coverScene: Iktomi the Trickster — a wiry man in a fringed deerskin shirt with a coyote-like grin — sitting cross-legged on a rock by a river, weaving a web of grass between his fingers while a group of prairie animals — a rabbit, a turtle, and a meadowlark — listen skeptically. Great Plains landscape, cottonwood trees along the river, wide golden light.
